entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Police fatally shoot man pointing replica gun in north suburban Highwood
entries.summary: <p>A Highwood police officer fatally shot a suicidal and intoxicated man who pointed a replica pistol at them on Thursday evening, authorities say.</p><p>Officers were called to a home in the northern suburb around 4:45 p.m. in the 200 block of Morgan Place to check on a man who was allegedly “highly intoxicated” and “suicidal,” according to a statement from the Lake County Major Crime Task Force.</p><p>The caller, a relative not at the home, said the man’s ex-girlfriend was inside with him. When officers arrived at the home, they learned the man had locked himself in the bathroom and tried to get him to come out, according to the statement. </p><p>The man told officers he was armed and he hoped they were wearing their bulletproof vests, the statement said. The man then burst out of the bathroom and pointed a replica pistol at the cops. One officer fired at him, according to the statement. </p><p>Police and paramedics on scene provided the man aid until he was taken to a nearby hospital, according to Lake County. The man, whose name was not released, was later pronounced dead.</p><p>Investigators with the Lake County Major Crime Task Force are conducting an independent investigation into the incident.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Police release photo of car involved in fatal March hit-and-run in South Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police released a photo and the license plate for the car involved in a fatal hit-and-run in South Chicago last month.</p><p>The car is a red 2022 Mitsubishi Outlander with the plates FE97346.</p><p>Police say the driver of that car <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/11/woman-killed-in-south-chicago-hit-and-run" target="_blank" >fatally struck a 32-year-old woman</a> on March 10 in the 2100 block of East 83rd Street. The driver fled the scene, according to police. </p><p>The woman was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where she later died. An autopsy on March 11 determined she died of multiple blunt force injuries from the crash, which was ruled an accident, according to the Cook County medical examiner's office.</p><p>Anyone with information should call CPD’s major accidents unit at 312-745-4521 or submit an anonymous tip at CPDTIP.com and use reference RD# JK-176843.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Adult son continues to make his mom an emotional hostage
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My wife recently immigrated to our current residence in the U.S. from Canada. She has a 23-year-old son dealing with mental illness back in Canada. He refuses to remain in therapy or to hold a steady job, and he failed out of university.</p><p>My wife consistently lowers her expectations and continues to support him. As a result, he has grown comfortable with emotionally manipulating everyone in his family while wallowing in self-pity. He is no longer allowed into our home because of his disrespectful and violent behavior, but he continues to punish his mom for moving on with her life. When does this end? — <i>GOING FORWARD IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR GOING FORWARD:</b> You and your wife have my sympathy. You both need a better way to cope with this sad reality. Her son is not going to change, and you cannot ignore him and pretend this will go away. Connecting with others who understand what you are going through can be an important source of support. An organization I have mentioned before in my column may guide you in the right direction. It's the National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI). Find out more by visiting nami.org.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a friend who comes to visit at my rural home. She's an alcoholic. My husband and I don't drink. Last time, she became so drunk that she was totally disoriented in my home. She couldn't find the bathroom or the bedroom. (We put her to bed.)</p><p>After she went home the next day, I stewed for a week and finally sent her a message that we could no longer host her, citing the reason as fear she would harm herself or others while driving here and possibly falling down my stairs, leading to a lawsuit.</p><p>I begged her to get help, and I messaged her son, asking if he could help her. Now, I have become the enemy! I have dealt with her for many years and watered down her alcohol every time she visited. Should we be done? — <i>OVERDOSED ON HER IN WYOMING</i></p><p><b>DEAR OVERDOSED:</b> Yes, you should be done — assuming she isn't already done with you. Your friend is an addict; she isn't going to change until she finds it absolutely necessary. You spoke the truth. Do not apologize for doing the right thing.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Three years ago, I lost my husband of 38 years to cancer. We lived in Southern California all our lives and enjoyed sightseeing and driving up and down the coast often. A year ago, I met "Allen," to whom I am now engaged. I miss all my beautiful seaside drives and the places I used to go with my late husband. Is it wrong to do those things with my new love? My grown children look at me funny when I say I went someplace with Allen that I used to visit with their father. — <i>MISSING MORE THAN THE MAN</i></p><p><b>DEAR MISSING:</b> Ignore the children. I don't think this is a question of right or wrong as long as Allen enjoys those trips down memory lane as much as you do. However, it might also be nice for you and Allen to plan some new adventures so you can create new memories together.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Abby shares more than 100 of her favorite recipes in two booklets: "Abby's Favorite Recipes" and "More Favorite Recipes by Dear Abby." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $16 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Cookbooklet Set, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Don't bash teens. Support them.
entries.summary: <p>My message to all those clutching pearls about the so-called <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/01/13/mayor-brandon-johnson-revised-curfew-ordinance-larry-snelling-brian-hopkins-teen-takeovers" >teen takeovers</a>: Leave those kids alone! This is, by any measure, the best "behaved" generation in history. They <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/02/02/gen-drinking-alcohol-chicagos-mental-health" >drink less</a> and even h<a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2025/07/11/nx-s1-5454738/gen-z-is-afraid-of-sex-and-for-good-reason" target="_blank" >ook up less </a>than any that came before them. Compared to any other group you can name, they are model citizens. Are the bankers of this city so "well-behaved"? Politicians? Clergy? Get real.</p><p>Rather than gnash their false teeth, the older generations should focus on providing safe outlets for young people to get up to some trouble, like music venues, all-ages clubs, parks and gyms. It's the older folks who've made this messed up world for the young. Worry about yourselves!</p><p><i>Jack Murphy, South Shore</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-140000" name="module-140000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Parks are great teenage hangout spots</h3><p>Teenagers are basically good people. The teen takeover phenomena is happening because they aren't hanging around the city parks like they did in the old days. Nobody really complained about them hanging out in public parks until boombox speakers were invented. If they hung out in parks, there would be no more teen takeovers in malls and Downtown shopping areas, and police wouldn't be dispatched for crowd control and to make arrests. Parks are great for teenagers who really need an outside space to be with other teenagers, and for the most part, they will regulate themselves. People living around parks have to realize that is what parks are intended to be and should not complain about noise.</p><p><i>Michael Zaczek, Plainfield</i></p><h3>City streets need makeover </h3> <p>As a Chicago grade school kid in the early 1960s, I remember what a grand thing it seemed to have all our alleys paved as part of Mayor Richard J. Daley's modernization initiative. The streets, for their part, were in great shape too, at least in my Northwest Side neighborhood. Near as I could tell then, this was truly the city that worked, as it was often billed.</p><p>Fast forward to today when I'm getting around with a walker and ranging out on foot to the city's far reaches from my adjoining suburb. I'm shocked to see the sad condition many streets are in now. So can they worry less about renaming them and more about their condition now that spring is here? I still love my hometown, but I'd love it more given some engineering improvements underneath my walker wheels.</p><p><i>Tom Gregg, Niles</i></p><h3>Medicare for All comes at a cost</h3><p>The Sun-Times opinion pages ran a lead letter over the weekend headlined, "<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/04/04/medicare-for-all-chicago-pope-taxes-voter-rights-save-act-teen-takeovers-loyola-murder-sox-cubs" >Medicare for all is a commonsense solution.”</a> But Medicare comes with premium payments, deductibles and limits for coverage. For example, the Part A deductible is $1736.00 for an inpatient hospital stay and the minimum Part B premium is $202.90 per month. Medicare for All would require massive subsidies from taxpayers, difficult legislation and a huge bureaucracy. People should have access to health care, but the devil is in the details.</p><p><i>Mike Cook, Bourbonnais</i></p><h3>Oval Office essential </h3><p>We definitely don’t need a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/washington/2026/03/31/trump-ballroom-judge-orders-construction-halted" >ballroom</a> in the White House. However, we do need a president!</p><p><i>Edwina Jackson,</i> <i>Washington Heights</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Watch out for college sports 'reforms' that shortchange student-athletes
entries.summary: <p>The world of college sports is at a crossroads. After years of legal challenges, cultural reckoning and economic change, student athletes finally are being recognized as central economic actors in a multibillion-dollar enterprise. Yet instead of embracing reforms that promote fairness and accountability, recent proposals from Washington D.C. would roll back progress and entrench inequity — at the direct expense of the very athletes who make college sports possible.</p><p>At issue are proposals that would sharply limit how much student athletes can earn from the value they help create, while expanding the power of institutions, conferences and regulators that already control nearly every aspect of college athletics. These efforts would shift billions of dollars away from players and back to coaches and administrators, many of whom already earn salaries that would dwarf anything athletes could see under the proposed rules.</p><p>One proposal would cap revenue sharing for athletes at roughly 22%, even as top football and basketball coaches continue to earn eight-figure annual salaries. The message is clear: the market should remain free for those at the top, but constrained for those whose labor actually generates the revenue. That is not reform — it is codified inequality.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-900000" name="module-900000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>An accompanying <a class="Link" href="https://www.espn.com/college-sports/story/_/id/48387866/executive-order-limits-ncaa-athletes-five-years-one-transfer" target="_blank" >executive action</a> doubles down on this imbalance. It gives a single private governing body sweeping authority to decide what constitutes “fair market value” for name, image, and likeness deals, known as NIL, and restricts athletes’ ability to enter into third-party agreements. The justification offered is that scholarships themselves are adequate compensation. But that logic rings hollow in a system where broadcast contracts, sponsorships and merchandise deals generate billions annually. Even more troubling, the policy threatens federal funding for institutions that fail to comply — jeopardizing vital programs such as Pell Grants that support low-income students across campuses nationwide.</p><p>These approaches do not exist in a legal vacuum. In fact, they directly defy a growing body of court decisions rejecting the long-standing treatment of student athletes as exempt from basic economic realities. In a unanimous decision, the Supreme Court ruled that restrictions on athlete compensation violated federal antitrust law. One justice went further, noting that “price-fixing labor is price-fixing labor,” and warning that the system would be illegal in almost any other industry. He also observed a central truth that reform opponents often ignore: the athletes generating the revenue are disproportionately Black and from lower-income backgrounds, and they have historically received “little or nothing” in return.</p><h3>Economic exploitation</h3><h3></h3><p>More recently, a federal appeals court rejected the notion that student athletes are categorically barred from being <a class="Link" href="https://www.williamsmullen.com/insights/news/legal-news/appellate-court-rules-ncaa-athletes-may-qualify-employees-under-fair-labor" target="_blank" >considered employees under labor law</a>. While that case continues, the court’s reasoning was unmistakable: when individuals provide tangible benefits to identifiable institutions, compensation is usually warranted. These rulings reflect a broader reality — college sports can no longer rely on legal fictions to justify economic exploitation.</p><p>Instead of adapting to this reality, current proposals seek to shield the system from accountability by granting an extraordinary antitrust exemption. Such exemptions are rare, and for good reason. Granting one here would allow unprecedented control over athletes’ earnings, mobility, and bargaining power, while sharply limiting their ability to challenge unfair practices in court. It would protect a system with a long history of mistreatment rather than reform it.</p><p>Equally alarming is the near-total absence of athlete representation in these plans. There is no meaningful pathway for student athletes to organize, negotiate collectively, or have a sustained voice in decisions that govern their livelihoods. That omission is not accidental. If athletes were allowed to bargain collectively, many of these rigid, top-down rules could instead be negotiated fairly, making sweeping federal intervention unnecessary.</p><p>True reform should strengthen college sports without sacrificing equity. That means fair revenue sharing, no special antitrust carve-outs, real representation for athletes and targeted support for smaller programs, women’s sports, and institutions that have historically been left behind, including Historically Black Colleges and Universities. Incremental tweaks are not enough if they reinforce the same disparities under a different name.</p><p>Most importantly, this debate must not move forward without the voices of those most affected. Current and former players, civil rights organizations and broader civil society deserve a meaningful seat at the table. Delaying action to allow for inclusive input is not obstruction — it is responsible governance.</p><p>College sports thrive because of student-athletes. Any reform that ignores their rights, their labor, and their humanity is not reform at all.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/marc-h-morial" target="_blank" ><i>Marc H. Morial</i></a><i> is president and CEO of the National Urban League</i> <i>and</i> <i>was mayor of New Orleans from 1994 to 2002.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-080000" name="module-080000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Marc H. Morial

entries.title: In ‘Windfall,’ Tarell Alvin McCraney asks if money buys justice for Black men
entries.summary: <p>Tarell Alvin McCraney slides into a cushy chair wearing a hoodie with “Trayvon” across the chest. That hoodie, as the 45-year-old Florida native will later reveal, is doing a bit of foreshadowing.</p><p>McCraney, the author of Steppenwolf’s world premiere play “Windfall,” is perhaps best known for his unreleased semi-autobiographical drama, “In Moonlight Black Boys Look Blue,” which was adapted for a theatrical release as “Moonlight.” It earned McCraney an Oscar in 2017 for co-writing the film’s script.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" data-module data-has-background ><a class="AnchorLink" id="custom-form-c10000" name="custom-form-c10000"></a><bsp-form class="Form-newsletter" data-form-container data-has-background style="--color-form-background: #e8dbed;; --color-form-link: var(--link-color); --color-form-link-hover: var(--color-link-text-hover); " data-ajax-submit="true"> <div class="Form-newsletter-logo"> <picture data-crop=""> <source type="image/webp" width="840" class="Form-newsletter-text-wrapper"> <div class="Form-newsletter-title"> Green Room Newsletter </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-submissionSuccess"> <p><b>You’re subscribed!</b></p><p></p><p>Please check your inbox for your confirmation.</p> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-description">Stay ahead of what’s hitting Chicago stages, galleries, museums and more with Green Room, WBEZ’s weekly arts & culture newsletter!</div> <div class="Form-newsletter-errorMessage"><p>Sorry, there was an error registering your email.</p></div> </div> <form data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label class="EmailInput-label"><span>Email</span> <input class="EmailInput-input" type="email" name="email" placeholder="name@email.com" required aria-required="true"> </label> </div> <button class="Button" type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Today, McCraney is fresh off a plane from Los Angeles where he’s the artistic director of the Geffen Playhouse. The sweatshirt he’s wearing pays homage to Trayvon Martin, the Florida teen who was fatally shot in 2012 by his neighbor while walking home from a corner store. The case garnered national attention.</p><p>The expectations for McCraney’s new play are huge. It stars four actors with recent Broadway runs and is the apex of Steppenwolf’s historic 50th season. The play also follows on the heels of “Purpose,” the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/03/18/purpose-broadway-hayes-theater-steppenwolf-phylicia-rashad-new-york-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>Branden Jacobs-Jenkins family drama</u></a> that went on to win<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/06/08/tony-awards-2025-broadway-purpose-steppenwolf-chicago-best-play" target="_blank" ><u> two Tonys</u></a> including Best Play.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b80000" name="module-b80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.steppenwolf.org/tickets--events/seasons-/2025-26/windfall/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Windfall</u></b></a></h3><br><b>When</b>: April 9 - May 31<br><b>Where</b>: Steppenwolf Theatre Co., 1650 N. Halsted St.<br><b>Info</b>: Tickets from $64.50</div> </div><p>On its face, “Windfall,” running through May 31 at Steppenwolf, is a play about money. Set in Chicago, the play follows a father who loses a child to police violence and stands to inherit a hefty sum from the government.</p><p>But underneath the unfortunately common story of a Black person being killed by police in America, sits a more complex examination of how money functions as religion. This is McCraney’s real creative sandbox.</p><p>“‘Windfall’ came from wanting to get in the room and have some courageous conversations about things like money, where money comes from and how we value it,” said McCraney, who describes himself as an “elder millennial.” “And how it's become a belief system. In the society that we live in, we often think of religiosity, or spirituality, as something intangible. But the American ethos has, for the past at least 100 years, made money a kind of tangible religion.”</p><p>McCraney offers an example, how society has normalized easing someone’s sadness or pain by giving them something of monetary value, like a GoFundMe after a tragedy. Or, when someone faces injustice, like police violence, “we think Justice can be rendered by a dollar amount,” he said.</p><p>“I know that to be true,” he continued, “because in our policies, in our cities across the country, if the city or the county has harmed you, there is a budget line item set aside specifically so that there’s some recompense. And I don’t know if that’s a bad thing, because, again, as a society, we value that.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-fullBleed> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-420000" name="image-420000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3fdfb6c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c7433e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d214dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tarell Alvin McCraney, playwright and Steppenwolf Theatre Ensemble member, at the Steppenwolf Theatre at 1650 N Halsted St in Lincoln Park, Thursday, April 9, 2026. Windfall written by Tarell McCraney will debut at the Steppenwolf Theatre. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d214dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/54235bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d214dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F27%2F759f52de4f3d89d0fa0d39fa17ed%2Fwindfall-260410-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“In the society that we live in, we often think of religiosity, or spirituality, as something intangible. But the American ethos has, for the past at least 100 years, made money a kind of tangible religion,” McCraney said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A cast of accomplished Steppenwolf ensemble members have reunited to bring these ideas to the stage. Artistic director Glenn Davis, Jon Michael Hill and Alana Arenas — a trio that all performed in “Purpose” both in Chicago and on Broadway — are joined by fellow ensemble Namir Smallwood, who is returning to Chicago after performing a Broadway production of Steppenwolf’s “Bug.”</p><p>Critics responded positively to “Bug,” with the “New York Times” describing Smallwood’s performance as “perceptive” and praising him for approaching the role with a “muted calm.”</p><p>Smallwood said he feels “spoiled” to return to his artistic home and join the cast of familiar faces. “It’s really been an exercise in patience and not getting attached. I really haven’t done a new play in a long time,” he said. “It’s a lot of moving parts, but Tarell is a genius.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-330000" name="image-330000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4729874/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff5f22b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/347bcf3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Carrie Coon and Namir Smallwood in Steppenwolf’s production of "Bug"" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/347bcf3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b263c12/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/347bcf3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+140/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5a%2Fbe%2F358057f64e68889c330ebbcb6227%2Fcarrie-coon-and-namir-smallwood-in-steppenwolfs-production-of-bug-michael-brosilow-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Namir Smallwood is returning to Chicago after performing a Broadway production of Steppenwolf’s “Bug” with Carrie Coon. Smallwood said he feels “spoiled” to return to his artistic home and join the cast of familiar faces.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The production puts McCraney’s mind on display. While the story is sparked from an instance of police violence, he approaches it with surrealism and a tone that may not be as heavy as one would imagine given the subject matter.</p><p>“I have a weird sense of humor, so I tend to laugh at things that I'm not supposed to,” he said. “So it was never even a question of whether or not there would be things that delight the senses.”</p><p>He also looks at art through the lens of the Church. “It’s amazing how 'The Passion of Christ' — the story of the crucifixion of Jesus Christ — “is put to song, to rhythm, to clapping, to jubilation,” he said. “And to me, that's how I've learned theatrically to move through tough conversations. You move through them with music. You move through them with rhythm.”</p><p>Arenas, who also grew up in Florida, attended the Marshall Davis African Heritage Cultural Arts Center in Miami with McCraney. From there, McCraney came to Chicago to study theater at DePaul University, where he met Davis. That opened up doors to the Steppenwolf ensemble, where he met Hill.</p><p>Along the way, McCraney has garnered a MacArthur “Genius” grant, a Tony nomination, a stint as the chair of the department of playwriting at Yale — and recently a job as artistic director at Geffen Playhouse. He’s also on a short list of Black writers who’ve won an Oscar.</p><p>Arenas said the playwright’s words land in a special way.</p><p>“When I read Tarell’s words, it feels like a nice garment that I put on,” she said. “It’s a comfortable fit. That’s a very nice experience.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-100000" name="image-100000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f14c0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a7c2c30/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7b0f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Windfall Rehearsal 15. Photo by Jeremy Williams.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7b0f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5428e75/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7b0f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+49/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F84%2F562de8254f829537ed800e2d59df%2Fwindfall-rehearsal-15-photo-by-jeremy-williams.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“When I read Tarell’s words, it feels like a nice garment that I put on,” actor Alana Arenas said. “It’s a comfortable fit. That’s a very nice experience.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Jeremy Williams</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In this production, the award-winning writer is using his words to foster a conversation at the intersections of capitalism, race and religion.</p><p>“When someone’s in grief, my first instinct isn’t to give them money,” he said. “But it may be the only thing that we can do. We don’t prioritize care. We don’t prioritize compassion. So what do we give people? What is the price? Then, when you start to look at the ways in which Black bodies, particularly, have been monetized, things get a little gnarly.”</p><p>McCraney’s hoodie is bringing awareness to Trayvon Martin. His new play, he hopes, will push forward the conversation around Black bodies and what justice on the backend of violence actually means.<br></p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Looking for a mentor in Chicago? Our illustrated zine has you covered
entries.summary:
Over the last few months, WBEZ higher education reporter Lisa Kurian Philip has been looking into the barriers that can keep young Black men from graduating college, and the kinds of support that can help them finish their degree.

She heard from researchers, professors, teachers and students themselves that having a mentor is one strategy that can make a big difference.

Some mentors provide academic guidance or help navigating complicated financial aid forms. Others can offer advice on choosing a major or a career. Still others can be there to listen when their mentee is at a crossroads and needs help thinking through next steps.

But she also heard that it can be hard for young people to find a mentor. It’s not always clear where to look, or how to make the initial ask.

So we turned to Charles Brown, a professor at Malcolm X College and a pastor in Austin on Chicago’s West Side, who has mentored many young people over the years. He coordinates a mentoring program at Malcolm X and has informally mentored young people through his church.

Brown shared with Kurian Philip his top tips for finding and connecting with a mentor, and WBEZ digital producer Mendy Kong turned that advice into a zine.

You can find a printable version of the zine here and below. (Never folded a zine? Learn here.)

We’ll also be distributing the zine in the Chicago area. Have a suggestion for where we should drop off some copies? Please email education editor Kalyn Belsha at kbelsha@chicagopublicmedia.org.

entries.title: Chicago cops did little to probe gun stolen from police station and used in three shootings, new records show
entries.summary: <p>When a valuable Glock handgun was stolen from inside a South Side police station, the Chicago Police Department did little to investigate, not even interviewing most of the officers who were there.</p><p>That was even after the gun had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/02/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-gresham-district-atf" target="_blank" >used in a series of shootings</a> and then was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/04/04/chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-michael-pfleger-gresham-district" target="_blank" >found near a teenage boy</a> who was caught trying to break in to a car, according to police records obtained by the Illinois Answers Project and Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>And it also was after a police supervisor told investigators he thought he knew what happened — that another cop had taken it.</p><p>Instead, investigators closed the case without figuring out who stole the gun, which had been turned in to the police and was supposed to have been destroyed.</p><p>After reporters asked how a gun could have been stolen from a police station full of cops, the department said it was reopening its investigation.</p><p>But, even then, investigators didn’t interview a single additional officer who was there when the weapon was swiped from the Gresham District station in December 2023.</p><p>And the police department fought to keep all of this secret, refusing to release records of the case that reporters sought under the Illinois Freedom of Information Act — doing so only after the publisher of the Illinois Answers Project took the city to court.</p><p>In the end, no one was charged or disciplined.</p><h3>Superintendent’s promise</h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/04/04/chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-michael-pfleger-gresham-district" target="_blank" >The gun was stolen</a> from a room that tactical team officers were using to inventory about 300 guns collected from a gun buyback event at St. Sabina Church in Auburn Gresham. A cop noticed that one of the guns — a .45-caliber Glock 21 — had gone missing, its inventory tag switched to another weapon.</p><p>Before the Glock was recovered nearly a year later, it had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/02/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-gresham-district-atf" target="_blank" >tied to three shootings</a> since the theft, including one in which <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/07/30/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-gresham-district" target="_blank" >a woman was wounded</a>. Police found the gun after chasing a 16-year-old who had been caught pulling on car door handles in November 2024.</p><p>The police department’s Bureau of Internal Affairs closed the initial investigation even before the gun was found.</p><p>When the case was reopened, investigators spoke to the two officers who arrested the boy found with the gun, but they said they remembered little about it.</p><p>Investigators tried but failed to interview the boy and attempted to find any social media connections between the teenager and Chicago police officers but came away with nothing. The second inquiry was closed after about five months, again without anyone charged.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-680001" name="image-680001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0162d2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a54879/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7d67a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A .25-caliber chrome handgun recovered at a Chicago police gun buyback on Dec. 2, 2023, was labeled with a tag for a .45-caliber Glock 21 that went missing from the Gresham District police station after the event. The tag for the chrome handgun was found on the ground. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7d67a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e83bb0a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c7d67a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2528x1419+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F38%2F20%2F49f683814ec181452646fecf265d%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-08-at-10-22-31-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A .25-caliber chrome handgun recovered at a Chicago police gun buyback event in December 2023 was labeled with a tag for a .45-caliber Glock 21 that was stolen from the Gresham District police station after the event. The tag for the chrome handgun was found on the ground.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The only officer initially disciplined in the case was a sergeant, Robert Brown, who approved the gun inventories. He was handed a one-day suspension. But that disciplinary action was reduced on appeal to a “violation noted” due to his “stellar” 23-year-history with the department. Brown won't comment.</p><p>For more than a year, the police department has refused to answer questions about its investigations into the theft of the gun, the shootings tied to it or any of the officers named in records related to the case.</p><p>One of the cops who was interviewed as a witness in the case was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/06/25/cpd-officer-krystal-rivera-funeral" target="_blank" >Officer Krystal Rivera</a>, who subsequently was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >shot and killed last summer</a> by her partner, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >Carlos Baker</a>, as they chased a suspect into an apartment building in what the police have said was an accidental shooting. Records show Rivera took steps to try to find the gun the day it was stolen. She wasn’t accused of any wrongdoing.</p><p>Asked a year ago about the stolen gun, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/larry-snelling" target="_blank" >police Supt. Larry Snelling</a> downplayed the theft, saying: “When you look at the number of guns that have been turned in, this is one case that is under investigation at this point. Once we get to the bottom of the investigation, if there’s anything nefarious that we find, we’ll take action.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-380000" name="module-380000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>A lengthy investigation goes nowhere</h2><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-e502e812-3371-11f1-9d0c-4faea0608c95"><li><b>Dec. 2, 2023:</b> A .45-caliber Glock 21 handgun is stolen from a room full of cops at the Gresham District station after being turned in at a buyback event. The Bureau of Internal Affairs subsequently opens an investigation.</li><li><b>Aug. 14, 2024:</b> The stolen gun is used in a shooting that leaves Twanda Willingham wounded near her Auburn Gresham home.</li><li><b>Sept. 19, 2024:</b> The gun is used in another shooting in South Shore that targeted Andre Williams and his mother Crystal Reynolds, who weren’t struck by the gunfire.</li><li><b>Sept. 26, 2024:</b> The investigation into the stolen gun is closed.</li><li><b>Oct. 13, 2024: </b>A car stolen from Auburn Gresham is recovered with shell casings inside that matched the stolen gun.</li><li><b>Nov. 30, 2024:</b> The gun is recovered during the arrest of a 16-year-old boy in South Shore.</li><li><b>Dec. 11, 2024:</b> A sergeant informs internal affairs that the stolen gun had been recovered and was used in the shootings. </li><li><b>Feb. 21, 2025:</b> Reporters from the Chicago Sun-Times and Illinois Answers Project ask the Chicago Police Department about the gun’s theft and the arrest of the teenage boy.</li><li><b>March 27, 2025:</b> Reporters send the police department a second inquiry about the theft and the arrest.</li><li><b>April 1, 2025:</b> The investigation into the gun’s theft is reopened.</li><li><b>June 5, 2025:</b> Officer Krystal Rivera, who helped inventory the buyback weapons and searched for the stolen Glock, is shot and killed by her partner during a foot chase in Chatham.</li><li><b>Sept. 9, 2025:</b> The investigation into the gun’s theft is closed for a second time.</li></ul></div> </div><p></p><h3>Who’s in charge?</h3><p>The police also haven’t charged anyone in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/02/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-gresham-district-atf" target="_blank" >the shootings tied to the stolen gun</a>.</p><p>In August 2024, a woman delivering food with her boyfriend was shot as they were parked near her Auburn Gresham home. </p><p>The following month, a 44-year-old woman and her 26-year-old son were fired upon as they saw a car burglary outside their South Shore apartment. </p><p>In October of that year, a woman’s car was stolen in Auburn Gresham, and eight days later she found it abandoned, damaged with gunshots, with shell casings inside that matched the stolen gun.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c00000" name="image-c00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eed823b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2522dfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d61bab7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Andre Williams (left) and his mother, Crystal Reynolds, outside their home in South Shore. The had dodged bullets fired from a gun that had been stolen from the Gresham District police station. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d61bab7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75a1bbb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d61bab7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fc3%2Fdae79f394de89e178dc6826c8842%2F1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Andre Williams (left) and his mother, Crystal Reynolds, outside their home in South Shore. They had dodged bullets fired from a gun that had been stolen from the Gresham District police station. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>By the time officers recovered the Glock in November 2024, the initial investigation into its theft had been closed without internal affairs having interviewed anyone besides Brown. He told them he wasn’t even in charge, but internal affairs disputed that.</p><p>Then-Cmdr. Michael Tate, who was responsible for running the Gresham District and appointing its tactical officers, wasn’t interviewed by internal affairs. He since has been promoted to deputy chief of the police department. He declined to comment.</p><p>Police reopened the internal investigation four months after the gun had been recovered and after discovering that it had been used in the shootings.</p><p>That was a year after Brown reported that he thought someone from the Gresham District’s 663 tactical team stole the gun.</p><p>“Why do you say the 663 team?” he was asked by Sgt. Hector Marino, the sole internal affairs investigator on the case, records show.</p><p>“Because they were in charge of these weapons, and they were responsible for inventorying them. And obviously some of them knew of this weapon as well," Brown said, according to a transcript of the interview.</p><h3>First investigation</h3><p>On Dec. 2, 2023, the day the Glock vanished, Brown was working essentially three jobs.</p><p>From inside the Gresham District tactical team office, he was working overtime, monitoring radio traffic for a tactical team that was on call for a Gaza war protest downtown.</p><p>He also was helping a separate tactical team inventory the hundreds of guns from the church buyback.</p><p>Those tasks were on top of his normal assignment, supervising a team that started at 6 p.m.</p><p>Records show the 663 team overseeing the bulk of the buyback duties didn’t have an assigned supervisor.</p><p>“No one in the … [Gresham] District, no matter what rank they are, gave me any instructions regarding this weapon turn-in,” Brown said, according to a transcript of his internal affairs interview. “No supervisor at any time, that’s over-ranked me, gave me any instructions on covering the 663 team, being their direct supervisor or approving or being told directly ‘approve their inventories.’ ”</p><p>“Yet you approved all 300 inventories,” Marino said, referring to the number of guns that had been turned in. “Why would you do that?”</p><p>“To help out because I was there. Not assigned. Because I was there,” Brown said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cb0000" name="image-cb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b37e982/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/31c5218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/38fd3ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A Glock handgun recovered at a gun buyback on Dec. 2, 2023, later went missing from the Gresham District station, 7808 S. Halsted St. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/38fd3ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a6ca052/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/38fd3ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F18%2F9f25c29946ce9fb81a26dc52eda7%2F4a46-x254-6510-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A Glock handgun recovered at a gun buyback event in December 2023 later went missing from the Gresham District station, 7808 S. Halsted St. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>He disputed the account of one of the police officers, Travis Betts, who told investigators that Brown told them to speed up the inventory process.</p><p>“I probably [told them] that they need to focus on what they’re doing,” Brown said, adding that the officers were talking about “irrelevant stuff.”</p><p>“I don’t remember telling them to speed it up because … 300 weapons is not going to be done in two minutes,” he said.</p><p>Brown said that, when he learned the gun was stolen, he informed two lieutenants who were in the station that day, Don Hoard and Ryan Doherty. Neither was interviewed by investigators.</p><p>In a separate interview with investigators, Betts said that other officers were admiring the Glock, a weapon commonly used in street violence, before it was stolen.</p><p>“Ah, yeah, it’s a nice one,” Betts said.</p><p>The two officers on the gun’s paperwork told investigators they didn’t know how their names got there and that they hadn’t filled out the forms.</p><p>Rivera, the officer who was fatally shot by her partner last summer, told Marino she looked in other officers’ bookbags once she realized the gun was missing. The police department has called Rivera’s shooting unintentional but hasn’t answered questions about the case.</p><p>While inventorying the guns, Rivera had said she prepared a report listing every gun that was recovered — a report that investigators said they couldn’t find.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e20001" name="image-e20001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e72a198/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f65cc27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Officer Krystal Rivera." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f7f3c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago police Officer Krystal Rivera after her graduation from the police academy in October 2021. Rivera was shot and killed by her partner, Carlos Baker, during a foot pursuit in June 2025. More than a year before that, in December 2023, she tried to find a gun that was stolen from the Gresham District police station.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Brown said the “computer system was messed up” and had trouble printing, possibly resulting in the report not being found.</p><p>Asked about a “security gap” that led to someone stealing the gun, Brown said the tactical team door was open because “people were coming in and out when they were going to the bathroom or whatever.”</p><p>Brown also said a janitor came into the room while the guns were being inventoried and emptied the garbage. But he said he “didn’t observe her even show any interest in these weapons.”</p><p>It’s not clear from the case file whether internal affairs investigators ever established that a janitor was working and, if so, who it was.</p><h3>Second investigation, same result</h3><h3> </h3><p>By the time a memo about the stolen Glock reached then-Internal Affairs Chief Yolanda Talley in September 2024, the gun had been used at least twice and was still out on the streets.</p><p>Just after Thanksgiving in 2024, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/04/04/chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-michael-pfleger-gresham-district" target="_blank" >police arrested the teenage boy</a> they said was seen checking car doors in South Shore. The boy ran when officers confronted him and ditched the gun, but officers found it a short time later.</p><p>When police checked the gun’s serial number, they realized it had been stolen from a neighboring district’s police station a year earlier. When they test-fired the gun and compared its shell casings against brass recovered at shooting scenes across the city, they discovered that it was tied to at least three other crimes since being stolen.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-b90000" name="html-embed-module-b90000"></a><iframe title="Journey of a Crime Gun" aria-label="Locator map" id="datawrapper-chart-OOAfA" src="https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/OOAfA/13/" scrolling="no" frameborder="0" style="width: 0; min-width: 100% !important; border: none;" height="729" data-external="1"></iframe><script type="text/javascript">!function(){"use strict";window.addEventListener("message",(function(a){if(void 0!==a.data["datawrapper-height"]){var e=document.querySelectorAll("iframe");for(var t in a.data["datawrapper-height"])for(var r,i=0;r=e[i];i++)if(r.contentWindow===a.source){var d=a.data["datawrapper-height"][t]+"px";r.style.height=d}}}))}(); </script> </div> </div><p>Records show top department officials knew for months that the gun had been used in the shootings before they decided to reopen the investigation into its theft.</p><p>In late February 2025 and again in late March, Illinois Answers Project and Sun-Times reporters sent questions to the police department about the gun and its disappearance.</p><p>Days later, the police reopened the internal investigation. Department officials later said it’s “inexcusable” that a gun had gone missing from police custody. Talley, who was chief of internal affairs at the time of the theft and during the initial investigation, had been promoted to first deputy superintendent. She retired last fall and didn’t respond to a request for comment.</p><p>After reopening the case, internal affairs didn’t reinterview anyone questioned in its initial investigation nor did they interview anyone else from the Gresham District.</p><p>When Brown’s union argued successfully for lesser discipline, it said Marino “did not conduct a thorough investigation” because he didn’t interview all the officers or review security camera footage. Marino couldn't be reached for comment.</p><p>During the second inquiry, the records show Marino tried to call the mother of the boy who was arrested with the gun. The boy was in custody on unrelated charges.</p><p>The records don’t show any attempt to contact the family in person.</p><p>Reporters had spoken with the boy and his mother at their home.</p><p>The boy’s mother told Marino she wasn’t sure how the boy obtained the gun, and Marino asked her to call back if her son wished to cooperate. It doesn’t appear she called back.</p><p>Marino called a detective assigned to one of the three cases tied to the shootings and told the detective the gun had been recovered. The detective told Marino his shooting investigation was suspended but called the teenager’s mother, who told him he couldn’t talk to the boy unless she was there.</p><p>The mother asked the detective: “Is this about that gun that was supposed to be in police custody?”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/04/04/chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-michael-pfleger-gresham-district" target="_blank" >‘Where is the Glock?’ Gun turned over to Chicago police wound up in the hands of a teenager</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/02/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-stsabina-gresham-district-atf" target="_blank" >Gun stolen from a roomful of Chicago cops ended up being used in a series of shootings</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/07/30/glock-chicago-police-department-gun-buyback-gresham-district" target="_blank" >Woman shot with Glock stolen from a Chicago police station sues city, says cops pilfered it</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Tom Schuba

entries.title: For some, Trump's deportation blitz last fall evokes painful memories of Latin American 'disappearances'
entries.summary: <p>In December 1979, Neris González was leaving the market in her farm town in central El Salvador when Salvadoran national guardsmen suddenly grabbed her and took her captive.</p><p>González, who was in her late teens, was taken to a clandestine prison where she was brutally tortured for two weeks before finally being released.</p><p>It was the beginning of the Salvadoran civil war, a bloody 12-year conflict with an estimated 5,000 forced disappearances according to a U.N. Truth Commission. For the duration of the war, González was separated from her two daughters, who were 1 and 7 years old at the time she was detained.</p><p>More than 45 years later, González, who moved to Chicago in 1997, thought she had seen the last of militarized patrols snatching people off the streets. But then U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol agents launched Operation Midway Blitz last fall, which González said brought back painful memories.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b10000" name="image-b10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d00164e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/903x507+0+309/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F0a%2F9059487140c79f5122be38776d52%2F88580412-cb02-48c6-b3a3-99ab9b9191a2-1.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="88580412-cb02-48c6-b3a3-99ab9b9191a2 (1).jpeg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d00164e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/903x507+0+309/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F0a%2F9059487140c79f5122be38776d52%2F88580412-cb02-48c6-b3a3-99ab9b9191a2-1.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ac199b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/903x507+0+309/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F0a%2F9059487140c79f5122be38776d52%2F88580412-cb02-48c6-b3a3-99ab9b9191a2-1.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d00164e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/903x507+0+309/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F0a%2F9059487140c79f5122be38776d52%2F88580412-cb02-48c6-b3a3-99ab9b9191a2-1.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Neriz González photographed in 1979.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Wow, incredible,” González, 70, said in Spanish, through an interpreter. “When I see Border Patrol here in Chicago, I never imagined ... that in a country called the United States and here in Chicago, there would be such an aggressive, violent, unaccountable and military migration response.”</p><p>That response included a military-style overnight raid of a South Shore apartment building with federal agents rappelling from a helicopter onto the roof, knocking down doors and rounding up 37 people, mostly Venezuelan migrants, but also a handful of U.S. citizens and children. Masked agents also deployed tear gas on residential streets, shot two people, killing one, and swept up people at courthouses, schools, gas stations, grocery stores, and work sites, often without warrants. After being arrested, an unreliable ICE locator system meant that some detainees effectively disappeared for days at a time. </p><p>The deportation campaign has been carried out with little oversight or transparency, said Ariel Dulitzky, an expert in the inter-American human rights system. </p><p>“There is a need to really go back and see — and not only going back, but in the present — what is happening with the detention of immigrants,” Dulitzky said.</p><p>That includes the detentions of American citizens, detention conditions, lack of protocol for ICE agents’ interactions with community members and ICE presence at schools, courthouses and churches, which conflict with constitutional rights.</p><p>“We need to do more documentation of what happened… even if there [are] less aggressive tactics today, [they] could be repeated in the future,” Dulitzky said.</p><p>In the meantime, the Illinois Accountability Commission, established by Gov. JB Pritzker, is working to hold the Trump administration accountable for potential human rights violations. Earlier this month, the commission requested testimony from top current and former officials responsible for Operation Midway Blitz as part of its ongoing probe.</p><p>Following the backlash to the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, Trump's mass deportation campaign has eased up of late. But in Chicago it has left a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/20/operation-midway-blitz-effects-latino-neighborhoods-pilsen-little-village-southeast-side" target="_blank" >lasting impact</a> on the city's Latino neighborhoods, where some residents still fear going outside.</p><p>And while caravans of masked agents aren't roaming the streets like they did during Midway Blitz, ICE remains a potent symbol and go-to resource for Trump. His administration has recently deployed ICE agents to airports nationwide, including O'Hare, in an attempt to bolster airport security.</p><p>For some, the events of the last year echo a dark chapter in Central and South American history, when tens of thousands of people were detained and disappeared. They worry that Trump's rhetoric and brand of immigration enforcement could eventually lead the U.S. down a similar path.</p><h3><b>‘</b>Nobody knew where she was’</h3><p>In early June of last year, Valentina Galvis and her then-7-month-old boy, Naythan, spent five days locked in a hotel guarded by private security contracted with ICE.</p><p>Galvis was apprehended by masked, plainclothes federal immigration agents as she held Naythan in her arms while leaving her check-in for her asylum case at a Downtown courthouse.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Valentina Galvis and her son Naythan at their home in Little Village. Valentina was detained for a week in a hotel by ICE after an immigration hearing. She was kept in a hotel without access to communication to the outside world before being released into the Department of Homeland Security’s Intensive Supervision Appearance Program office and allowed home under supervision. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/08/15/ice-contractor-locked-immigrant-baby-ohare-hotel-five-days" target="_blank" >reported by Injustice Watch</a>, she and her husband, Camilo Arias, moved to the U.S. together in 2022 after fleeing violence in their home country of Colombia. They had also obtained U.S. work authorization, according to her lawyer, William McLean.</p><p>Galvis and Naythan were driven to the ICE processing center in Broadview and were told they would be sent to a family detention center in Texas before being deported. She was allowed a brief phone call to her husband, who is a truck driver and was in Boston at the time.</p><p>Galvis feared Naythan would be forced to grow up without his mother and that she would be permanently separated from her husband. </p><p>“It was terrible because I always, always, thought I was going to be deported — that I didn’t have another option,” Galvis said, through an interpreter. “...Practically my family was going to be destroyed. I was scared... about returning to Colombia. I was scared for my son… for my husband… for my family most of all.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Valentina Galvis’ son Naythan at their home in Little Village. Valentina was detained for a week in a hotel by ICE after an immigration hearing. She was kept in a hotel without access to communication to the outside world before being released into the Department of Homeland Security’s Intensive Supervision Appearance Program office and allowed home under supervision.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The mother and son were driven to a hotel near O'Hare Airport, where a federal agent immediately unplugged and removed the hotel room phone. They stayed there for one night and at another nearby hotel for four days.</p><p>During all this time, her lawyer said, the ICE online locator system, meant to be a key tool for lawyers and families to find people in the immigration detention system, inaccurately showed Galvis was being held in Washington, D.C., where no ICE detention center exists.</p><p>“Nobody knew where she was," said McLean, her lawyer. "I didn’t find out what the hotel was until I showed Valentina an image of all these hotels around O’Hare and she said, ‘That’s it, that’s the one right there.’ Completely cut off from me, completely cut off from her husband.”</p><p>“It’s the worst thing I have ever seen in my entire career — either that I’ve been involved in or that I’ve heard about or read about," McLean said. “Considering my client’s equities — three, four years in the U.S., work authorization, valid asylum case, her U.S. citizen child in her arms, that’s probably what makes it the worst. And also the context of where they held her."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a10000"
