class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A press release for the fatal hit and run of Violet Harris indicates that Crime Stoppers is offering a reward of up to $1000 for information that leads to the arrest of the driver.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We urge anyone with information to come forward and help bring justice to Violet and her family” Executive Director of Cook County Crime Stoppers Paul Rutherford said in a Friday press release. “Your information may be the piece needed to bring the family justice.”</p><p>Major Accident Investigators are continuing to investigate the incident. Authorities told the public not to approach or attempt to apprehend any individual believed to be involved. Those with information are encouraged to report it anonymously.</p><p>“We are seeing a troubling spike in fatal hit-and-run incidents across our communities,” Rutherford said. “It is a shame that drivers are not taking responsibility and stopping to do the right thing. What may begin as an accident quickly escalates into a serious felony when someone chooses to flee the scene.”<br></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Kenya Merritt’s DCASE appointment advances as alderpersons voice concerns about whistleblower lawsuit
entries.summary: <p>Kenya Merritt’s appointment to be Chicago’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/03/19/chicago-arts-kenya-merritt-cultural-affairs-dcase-commissioner-grants" target="_blank" ><u>next cultural commissioner</u></a> advanced out of a City Council committee Thursday, but a <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/city-hall/2026/03/23/chicago-dcase-deputy-whistleblower-lawsuit-kenya-merritt-wrongful-termination" target="_blank" ><u>whistleblower lawsuit</u></a> filed against Merritt and the city by a former top deputy dominated much of the hours-long questioning from alderpersons.</p><p>The lawsuit was filed last month in Cook County Circuit Court by Rosalyn Kimberly (“Kim”) Grigsby, the cultural department’s former first deputy, alleging that her February termination was in retaliation for filing complaints about alleged wrongdoing within the department to the city’s inspector general and Department of Human Resources.</p><p>Earlier this week, lawyers for Grigsby announced they had amended the complaint, adding allegations that Merritt made false statements about Grigsby to City Council members ahead of the confirmation process.</p><p>Grigsby, herself a lawyer, began in her role at the Department of Cultural Affairs and Special Events last summer. In a statement, her attorney said, “This case is about accountability. When public employees speak up about misconduct, they should be protected — not punished.”</p><p>The lawsuit is the latest development in what has been a tumultuous period at DCASE, which oversees large Downtown events and administers millions of dollars in arts grants annually.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson selected Merritt to be interim commissioner <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/city-hall/2025/10/10/dcase-commissioner-cultural-affairs-mayor-brandon-johnson-taps-longtime-city-employee-kenya-merritt" target="_blank" ><u>last October</u></a>, after his first <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2025/10/07/chicago-cultural-commissioner-dcase-clinee-hedspeth-resigns-city-hall" target="_blank" ><u>hand-picked cultural commissioner resigned</u></a> amid allegations of bullying and <a class="Link" href="https://www.nbcchicago.com/news/local/former-dcase-commissioner-resigned-after-hr-investigation-harassment-allegations/3847000/" target="_blank" ><u>sexual harassment</u></a>.</p><p>At the Thursday meeting of the City Council’s cultural committee, many alderpersons and arts leaders praised Merritt’s long tenure with the city and her leadership of the department in recent months. But, over a period of more than two hours, several Council members questioned whether the pending litigation was part of a larger pattern, which has led to the ouster of other <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/19/mayor-johnson-deputy-mayor-of-public-safety-garien-gatewood-firing-toxic-work-environment" target="_blank" ><u>senior Johnson officials</u></a> in recent months.</p><p>“We've seen some pretty serious patterns with this administration in terms of really pervasive, destructive, unreasoned behavior in a lot of departments at commissioners level,” said Ald. Scott Waguespack (32nd).</p><p>Ald. Samantha Nugent (39th) added that the lawsuit “raises serious concerns about how leadership within the department has operated.”</p><p>Ald. Anthony Napolitano (41st) lobbied for delaying the vote until after the lawsuit is settled, saying, “if we vote right now for her and her and the department are found guilty, we're going to look like we didn't do our due diligence as Council people.”</p><p>During the meeting, both Merritt and a representative from the city’s Law Department repeatedly said they could not comment on pending litigation.</p><p>The hearing began with a long list of high-profile arts leaders voicing their enthusiastic support for Merritt’s nomination. Among them was <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts/2025/11/13/nora-daley-richard-m-daley-chicago-arts-culture-architecture-biennial" target="_blank" ><u>Nora Daley</u></a>, chair of the Illinois Arts Council, who called Merritt “a proven advocate for Chicago's arts and cultural community.” Additional support came from leaders of prominent venues from the Goodman to the Harris, as well as former DCASE commissioner Mark Kelly.</p><p>“I've known Kenya for over 10 years. I can say without question that she will bring the authority to right the ship of DCASE,” said Kelly, who led the cultural department from 2016 until his retirement in 2021. “Kenya will bring the care and leadership to support the great, talented DCASE staff and Kenya is going to remind all of us that one of the reasons this city is so great is because of its cultural vitality.”</p><p>Sonia Lopez, a union steward with AFSCME Local 2912, representing the unionized DCASE employees, also provided public comment, saying, “Our members were optimistic about [Merritt’s] appointment, given she understands DCASE, and they have had positive interactions with her. However, they feel strongly since they’re still reeling from the environment created by the last commissioner that new leadership needs to address issues in human resources for the culture to change.”</p><p>“There is a pattern of even basic requests going unanswered for weeks, and when responses do come, they are often dismissive or passive aggressive instead of helpful,” Lopez said. “We hope the Council encourages the nominee to take these concerns seriously so we can truly turn the corner on bad leadership at DCASE.”</p><p>For her part, Merritt said she is committed to engaging in ongoing dialogue with unionized staff, and that she is focused on “establishing stronger internal norms that prioritize communication, accountability and team development.”</p><p>As was frequently noted Thursday by both Merritt and her supporters, she has had a long tenure with the city across administrations, serving in a variety of roles, including a former stint as DCASE’s chief financial officer and interim chief operating officer.</p><p>“What I will say for me is that I’ve worked for the city for 26 years in multiple roles, multiple politically appointed roles, and I’ve never had any issues with my staff,” Merritt said in the meeting. “I take it seriously around the culture that I set around the work that we do, how we show up and I’ll continue to do that in this role.”</p><p>Merritt’s appointment now advances to the full Council, which will meet Wednesday.</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Chicago murals: Native wildflowers in Forest Glen mural painted by a native son
entries.summary: <p>When Wesley Truman Daniel visits his old Sauganash neighborhood, or his parents travel to O’Hare Airport, they drive under the highway overpass at North Forest Glen Drive, west of North Cicero Avenue.</p><p>So when Forest Glen Community Club members requested proposals for a mural in that underpass, the Chicago Public Schools elementary art teacher submitted not just one, but four different designs.</p><p>“I was raised in Sauganash,” says Daniel, who now lives in Buena Park. “This is a big one.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" name="image-8f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f36e462/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6387x3585+0+338/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd5%2Fb9%2F379df88844e69de1292ae3264c4c%2Fmurals-forestglen-260412-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Artist Wesley Truman Daniel used to live in Sauganash.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The team selected one of those designs, titled Dawn Flower Forest, to cover one of the walls under Interstate 94 on the border of Sauganash and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/forest-glen" >Forest Glen</a>. He painted it last fall.</p><p>The mural shows native Illinois flowers like black-eyed Susans as tall as the underpass, resembling a forest of flowers. Residents run, bike and push a child on a tire swing among the stems that stand on rolling hills of green. Shades of blue and puffy white clouds billow behind it all.</p><p>“It was my wife’s idea to make (flowers) forest-sized and have (residents) strolling through,” Daniel says.</p><p>He got a boost from Chicago artist Cyd Smilie, president of Arts Alive Chicago, a nonprofit in Old Irving Park that promotes neighborhood art, connects artists, businesses and property owners and supports neighborhood arts initiatives. Daniel served as an intern for Smilie when he was a student at nearby Northeastern Illinois University, and the two continued working together after he finished his program.</p><p>When Forest Glen Community Club members asked Arts Alive Chicago staff to recruit artists to submit proposals, Smilie encouraged Daniel to apply. He was one of six to submit ideas, she says.</p><p>“We try to bring in artists who meet the style and standard they’re interested in,” Smilie says. The Forest Glen Community Club wanted a mural that included nature, neighbors and the active lifestyle that draws many to the neighborhood. They didn’t want it to reflect current events or anything else that would date the mural as it aged.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28755ff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa1%2F47%2F0b753ec845538ad6416b22125f19%2Fmurals-forestglen-260412-012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The mural shows native Illinois flowers like black-eyed Susans as tall as the underpass, resembling a forest of flowers.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>With its active subjects, native flowers and vibrant hues, Daniel’s proposal fit the bill.</p><p>Smilie helped Daniel as he painted the mural last fall. He crafted his design with the app Procreate, and Smilie brought her virtual reality glasses so the two could plug the design into the glasses, look at the wall and then trace its outline, he says. They hand-painted the rest with acrylic house paint.</p><p>The mural was so well received that the club commissioned Daniel to paint another across the street in the same underpass. This mural will include a forest of native flowers, this time in the evening with neighbors slowing down under a moon, stars and rich purple night sky, he says. He plans to paint that one this summer.</p><p>Smilie says the final mural was just what she hoped for. “It’s so clean and colorful. It’s exactly what Forest Glen wanted.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-fullBleed> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ce0000" entries.title: What's a clear sign of spring in Chicago? Here's what you told us
entries.summary: <p>We asked readers what's a clear sign of spring in Chicago? Here's what you told us, lightly edited for clarity:</p><p>"Tulips come up on Michigan Avenue."<br><i>— Tara Goodwin</i></p><p>"Construction during peak travel times."<br><i>— Brian Althimer</i></p><p>"The return of the piping plovers to Montrose Beach Sanctuary signals abundant insects, warm sunshine for tiny feathered bodies and nature's symphony ready for another beautiful season."<br><i>— Toni Scavo</i></p><p>"Full tour boats."<br><i>— Sue Wynn</i></p><p>"Automobiles with tickets for not moving during assigned street cleaning."<br><i>— Edward M. Bury</i></p><p>"I don’t have to wear my skull cap for two weeks straight."<br><i>— George Wilson</i></p><p>"Potholes."<br><i>— Christopher Vaughn</i></p><p>"When the weather gets off the roller coaster and stays in the upper 60s for more than three days."<br><i>— Scott Wisniewski</i></p><p>"Allergies."<br><i>— Glennie Griffin</i></p><p>"When I see worm castings. It's when they surface for the first time after staying under during winter."<br><i>— Michael Blane</i></p><p>"Ivy turning green at Wrigley."<br><i>— Rick Gross</i></p><p>"When you hear those four words in the forecast: 'cooler near the lake.'"<br><i>— DeeDee Kotowski-Keacher</i></p><p>"Lots of robins singing their little hearts out."<br><i>— Lidia Poulo</i></p><p>"The city crews are out planting all the flower boxes."<br><i>— LeAnn M. Schneider Hibler</i></p><p>"Delusional Cubs fans replace delusional Bears fans."<br><i>— Tim Zeilstra</i></p><p>"The boats coming out of winter storage."<br><i>— Kelly Carter</i><br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Troubled Skokie woman lied about being detained by ICE for more than a day, Wisconsin sheriff says
entries.summary: <p>A troubled Skokie woman with a history of lying to the police engineered an elaborate “hoax” to make it appear as if she’d been detained by federal immigration agents for more than a day, a Wisconsin sheriff told reporters Friday.</p><p>During a news conference, Dodge County Sheriff Dale Schmidt presented evidence that he said exposed Sundas “Sunny” Naqvi’s web of lies about being <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/08/skokie-woman-citizen-sunny-naqvi-detained-customs-border-patrol-ohare-immigration-ice" target="_blank" >detained last month</a> at the county jail and a suburban U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility.</p><p>Schmidt said he couldn’t charge Naqvi with any crimes in Wisconsin, but he’d referred the matter to other law enforcement agencies, including the FBI and Illinois State Police.</p><p>He also announced that he filed a federal defamation lawsuit against her and Cook County Commissioner Kevin Morrison, who was among the political candidates and representatives who spoke out about Naqvi’s purported detention as a U.S. citizen. He’s seeking $1 million.</p><p>Last month, Morrison joined Naqvi’s family and other supporters when they brought attention to her alleged detention. They told reporters that Naqvi and five colleagues had been detained by federal immigration agents at O’Hare Airport while returning from an overseas work trip.</p><p>They said Naqvi, 28, and her co-workers were then taken to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Broadview before Naqvi was transferred in ICE custody to Dodge County Jail in Wisconsin, where ICE detainees are held. They even shared screenshots that purportedly showed she had been taken to both facilities.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-190000" name="image-190000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6b97ce8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ed80799/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/883a14c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Sarah Afzal, sister of “Sunny” Naqvi, speaks about the detainment of her sister outside of the Broadview Immigration Detention Facility in Broadview, Sunday, March 8, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/883a14c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/79f4af8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/883a14c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Ff2%2F8174d2d24bd6aba544caa791b000%2Fsunnybroadview-030925-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sarah Afzal, sister of Sundas “Sunny” Naqvi, claimed that her sister was detained by federal immigration officials during a news conference outside of the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Broadview last month. Officials have since said there’s no evidence corroborating Sunny Naqvi’s story.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But it was all a lie, according to Schmidt.</p><p>He shared text messages, surveillance footage and hotel records indicating Naqvi went from the airport to a Rosemont hotel before asking someone to drive her to Wisconsin, where she “reunited” with her family.</p><p>Cellphone location records showing she was inside the jail were likely spoofed, Schmidt said.</p><p>“I don't have an answer for why you would do something like that,” Schmidt said. “I could hypothesize, just as all of us could. There's a hypothesis: maybe it's just to go after President Trump. Maybe it's just to go after ICE.</p><p>“Maybe it's because it's something she enjoys doing.”</p><p>Naqvi’s story had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/11/ice-detention-sundas-sunny-naqvi-ohare-department-homeland-security" target="_blank" >already started to unravel</a> after it made headlines and her history of deception was exposed.</p><p>The U.S. Department of Homeland Security previously released images showing Naqvi leaving a secure area at O’Hare, just an hour after she was flagged for screening.</p><p>The Cook County sheriff’s office poked another hole in her account, saying she wasn’t found during a search of the Broadview facility.</p><p>And the multinational software company she claimed she worked for said she’d never been hired, and that none of its employees had been detained at O’Hare.</p><p>Naqvi didn’t respond to a request for comment. Neither did Morrison or Naqvi’s onetime lawyer, Robert Held.</p><p>Schmidt’s broadside marks the latest example of Naqvi facing allegations she falsely presented herself as a victim. Records obtained by the Sun-Times show Naqvi has repeatedly been charged with filing false police reports.</p><p>In one case, filed in Cook County court, she was convicted of lying about being the victim of a violent sexual assault and was sentenced to two years probation.</p><p>Naqvi claimed that stab wounds she had on her thighs were sustained during the alleged attack near her home in Skokie. But in the end, Skokie police found she had falsely accused an ex-boyfriend of assaulting her, and instead brought a felony case against her.</p><p>Skokie police found the ex-boyfriend had actually been “catfished” on a dating app and drawn to the Evanston-Chicago border around the time of the alleged attack, likely by Naqvi.</p><p>But it wasn’t the first time Naqvi was accused of making a bogus report — or breaking the law.</p><h3><b>Series of criminal charges</b></h3><p>In May 2019, Naqvi and her roommate at University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign were accused of holding the same ex-boyfriend at knifepoint, according to police reports.</p><p>She was accused of pushing the boyfriend to delete evidence off his phone and computer related to allegations she’d made against a professor she accused of sexually harassing her and trading sex for better grades, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wandtv.com/news/ui-professor-admits-sharing-photos-denies-allegations-of-sexual-favors/article_aff5fec0-7288-11e9-9b56-2fff8085d87f.html" target="_blank" ><u>according to media reports</u></a>. The professor stepped down but denied wrongdoing.</p><p>Naqvi was charged with intimidation and aggravated unlawful restraint, court records show.</p><p>About a week later, Naqvi reported the ex-boyfriend broke into her apartment, stole pills and left a note admitting to making up the earlier incident, according to a Champaign police report. She allegedly asked police if she’d be allowed to graduate that weekend if they arrested the ex-boyfriend.</p><p>When officers found the ex-boyfriend, he said he’d been in meetings with the school’s dean all day, then spent the rest of the evening with his parents, records show. Naqvi was eventually charged with making a false report.</p><p>In January 2020, Naqvi reported her car was stolen, but she was again charged with falsifying that report.</p><p>Naqvi was never convicted in any of the cases filed in Champaign County court, records show.</p><p>Naqvi has also been evicted twice in recent years, Cook County court records show. In 2024, a River North apartment complex said she owed over $43,000 in rent and fees. Around the same time, JP Morgan said she owed nearly $15,000 in unpaid credit card debt.</p><p>Schmidt, the Dodge County sheriff, said Naqvi had roped a new man into subsidizing her lifestyle and paying $12,000 for the trip she took to Turkey before she claimed she was detained. That same man drove Naqvi to Wisconsin but ultimately began cooperating with the sheriff’s office after seeing media reports about the alleged detainment, according to Schmidt.</p><div alt="Sundas "Sunny" Naqvi was pictured shopping at a gas station market in Wisconsin in March 2026, around the time she claimed she was detained by federal immigration officials." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75d960a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+269/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb7%2Fc30182814910a339de18072d18af%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-3-48-26-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/24eab4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+269/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb7%2Fc30182814910a339de18072d18af%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-3-48-26-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75d960a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+269/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb7%2Fc30182814910a339de18072d18af%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-3-48-26-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sundas “Sunny” Naqvi was pictured shopping at a gas station market in Wisconsin last month, around the time her supporters claimed she was released by federal immigration officials.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Dodge County sheriff’s office</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The man told sheriffs he gave Naqvi about $25,000 in a month “because he thought there was a very long term relationship in their future,” Schmidt said.</p><p>The man reported that he was charged $1,000 for a law firm on a day Naqvi said she was detained – a charge he didn’t authorize, according to Schmidt.</p><p>Schmidt said Naqvi “continues to do this and get away with it, and she has other people who are helping to do that.”</p><p>“That's what this is about, making sure that people know that he, and people like her are out there, and making sure that it doesn't happen again,” Schmidt added.</p><div entries.title: Signing day preview: Could the Sky actually turn it around?
entries.summary: <p>The Sky were supposed to be left for dead. No more Angel Reese, traded to Atlanta for future draft picks, and no clear path forward.</p><p>Just three players remain on their roster after the Reese deal — Kamilla Cardoso, Maddy Westbeld and Hailey Van Lith — none of them All-Stars.</p><p>But the rumor mill is whispering about a resurrection.</p><p>One report says seven-time <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/robertawbb" target="_blank" >All-Star Skylar Diggins is in talks with the Sky.</a></p><p>The Sun-Times has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/09/how-many-balloons-does-it-take-to-woo-a-star-and-the-skys-other-pressing-free-agency-questions" >skeptical that Diggins will ultimately choose Chicago</a>, as the Sky have pursued her before without success. She's arguably the top point guard on the market and likely has other suitors willing to offer a max contract.</p><p>Why would a competitor like Diggins join a team coming off back-to-back losing seasons?</p><p>But maybe she sees something in Chicago. She did grow up in South Bend, Indiana, just 90 miles southeast of the city. </p><p>For Sky fans who've had a tough week, the report is worth a little hope.</p><p>Landing Diggins could reshape the team's entire outlook. She averaged 15.5 points and 6 assists last season — a premier point guard who can elevate her teammates and close games herself. She is also a recognizable star, the kind Chicago rarely lands in free agency.</p><p>The Sky are also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/09/sky-reportedly-in-talks-to-trade-ariel-atkins-for-rickea-jackson" >reportedly in talks with the Sparks to trade guard Ariel Atkins for Rickea Jackson</a>, a dynamic scorer and the No. 4 pick in the 2024 draft.</p><p>If both moves come together, it gives the franchise a sense of direction. Suddenly there's a Diggins-Jackson-Cardoso trio to build around. If the Sky retain their No. 5 pick and draft well, that's even more momentum.</p><p>None of it will erase the loss of Reese. That trade will reverberate for years — because of her connection to fans, because of the draft capital the Sky surrendered to get her, because of what it says about the franchise's ability to develop and retain young stars.</p><p>It also doesn't fully resolve their long-term future, particularly at point guard. Diggins is 35, and Courtney Vandersloot is 37.</p><p>While many star signings were reported around the league Friday, the only Sky news was that backup center Elizabeth Williams will sign a two-year deal with the team. </p><p>Saturday is the first day free agents can ink their contracts after an extremely condensed negotiating period — and it should be more revealing.</p><p>For the Sky, consider it judgment day.</p><p><b>Dream free agency update</b></p><p>Meanwhile, in Atlanta, the Dream are locking down their core. Per ESPN, All-WNBA guard Allisha Gray is signing a three-year deal with the team.</p><p>They are also bringing back four-time All-Star Brionna Jones on a multi-year deal and Sixth Player of the Year Naz Hillmon on a three-year deal. Both will be Reese's partners in the frontcourt.</p><p>If the Dream also retain guards Rhyne Howard and Jordin Canada, they'll be poised to continue their rise. The team jumped from the middle of the pack in 2023 and 2024 to a second-place regular season finish last season. Reese could help push them over the top. </p><p><b>Recent announcements and reports from around the league</b></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-70313c20-351b-11f1-9810-4f242c66df94"><li>Kelsey Mitchell returns to the Fever</li><li>Arike Ogunbowale returns to the Wings</li><li>Brittney Sykes to sign with the Tempo</li><li>Nneka Ogwumike to sign with the Sparks</li><li>Jackie Young returns to the Aces</li><li>Alyssa Thomas returns to the Mercury</li><li>Natisha Hiedeman to sign with the Storm</li><li>Jewell Loyd returns to the Aces</li><li>Satou Sabally is leaving the Mercury</li></ul>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Silver Room Block Party returning to new venue: The Salt Shed
entries.summary: <p>After a two year hiatus, the Silver Room Sound System Block Party is returning this summer to a new venue not far from its roots.</p><p>The dance music party, which has been scaled back to one day, is <a class="Link" href="https://www.silverroomblockparty.com/" target="_blank" ><u>scheduled</u></a> for noon - 10 p.m. on July 18 at The Salt Shed, 1357 N. Elston Ave. Tickets are on sale April 17.</p><p>Founded in 2002 by local entrepreneur Eric Williams as a grassroots alternative to Chicago’s slate of summer street festivals, the event has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2023/7/26/23808895/chicago-silver-room-block-party-final-year-2023-music" target="_blank" >gone through many changes</a> over the years.</p><p>It has long been celebrated by loyal fans of house music, which was founded in Chicago. The parties were powered by local artists and were solely about the music and dancing.</p><p>"It was no alcohol, no drugs, just music," former Block Party volunteer Rebecca McQuillen previously told the Sun-Times. "During that time, we would get shut down by the police because they couldn’t believe that there was no alcohol."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2002) came from a festival in Germany that lasted all night long and showcased city culture.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided / Silver Room Block Party</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The block party began in a Wicker Park alley near the art and fashion store Silver Room. The party grew over the ensuing decade, moving to a stretch of Evergreen Avenue and reaching more than 8,000 attendees by 2014.</p><p>The party shifted locations to Hyde Park in 2016, where it eventually attracted more than 40,000 attendees before the COVID-19 pandemic put a two-year pause on the event.</p><p>The event returned in 2022 as a two-day, ticketed festival at Oakwood Beach. It was held there again in 2023 where it was billed as the festival’s finale.</p><p>Williams said in 2023 that difficulties making a profit and the amount of work involved in setting up the festival led to his decision to “lay this to bed” after a “good run.”</p><p>He couldn't immediately be reached for comment on Friday.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: As Reinsdorf meeting looms, Bulls coach Billy Donovan should run — and run fast!
entries.summary: <p>Run, Billy! Just freakin’ run!</p><p>Like your last name was Gump, like O.J. in airports and in white Broncos, like DMC was your partner, like Ryan Poles gave you a contract to replace D’Andre Swift and Kyle Monangai, like Sha’Carri Richardson would be afraid to line up against you, as if your career depended on how fast you got away.</p><p>Run fast, run hard, run far. Then run farther. Distance yourself from this organization like the entire UK did Ye. Yeah, Jesus walked but you, my friend, got a whole other kinda devil trying to bring you down.</p><p>With the rumors (so many rumors) in both the pubic and social spaces of what’s going to go down in next week’s meeting between <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="_blank" >Bulls</a> coach Billy Donovan and ownership — everything from convincing him to stay as coach as they seek replacements of the second of their failed GM/EVP combo to possibly offering him both aforementioned positions with him having a primary role in selecting who actually replaces him as head coach — a sense of forlorn urgency creeps upon the situation for Donovan.</p><p>Don’t pay attention to a thing CEO <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/04/08/bulls-team-president-michael-reinsdorf-boxes-checked-next-hire" target="_blank" >Michael Reinsdorf said Wednesday</a>, don’t listen to a damn thing they pitch to you next week. Run!</p><p>A family that purchased the Bulls (for a little over $9 million) the year after it drafted Michael Jordan (1985) with money the patriarch had acquired through co-founding a real-estate company (Balcor) and selling it to American Express (1994) that is currently valued at over $5 billion has, if you removed Jordan’s Era and the five-season Derrick Rose/Tom Thibodeau Era that followed a generation later, operated honestly at an F-minus functionality level in relation and comparison to most other teams in the NBA. Those two eras for the most part have saved the Bulls.</p><p>But neither play any role in where the franchise as a whole is or what its ownership group has in mind moving forward. “Too little, too late” seems to be the ecumenical<b> </b>sentiment of their latest move to finally clean out the front office. “Privileged apathy” seems to be the overwhelming sentiment of how and why we got here. Both sentiments point directly to the people who will be in control of the meeting with Donovan and his potential future.</p><p>This is why “Run, Billy, Run” should be the song The Temptations sequel their “Charlie” classic with.</p><p>(Don’t you just love when music lyrics imitate life?)</p><p>Honestly, I’d never been a fan of Donovan’s during much (probably closer to half) of his tenure as Bulls coach. That was until what was exposed over the last two seasons, when we all got to see the obvious: “Damn, this ain’t Billy’s fault at all!” When we realized that what he was <i>really</i> dealing with, when we collectively began to feel sorry for him.</p><p>In a sense (outside of Patrick Williams) Donovan should be considered the Last Samurai in all of this, when it all goes down. Walk into the meeting, confront the Emperor (Jerry if there, Michael if not) with the proverbial sword, sacrifice the life of his future with the franchise while rejecting their offer to remain, disappear into the NBA unknown until the Knicks fire Mike Brown.</p><p>Just don’t be stuck in an Advocate Center office after practice next season listening to the Doobie Brother’s “Minute By Minute,” replaying Michael McDonald’s lyric of “You think I’m your fool/Well, you may just be right” over and over and over again. My guy: They aren’t worth your torture.</p><p>John Hollinger’s point in the Athletic about “Bulls’ GM hire won’t matter if they aren’t honest with themselves” is valid. It’s just a matter of what “honesty” honestly looks like to them. Because if we’re being honest … as Zach Lowe said one NBA executive said to him when speaking about the executive openings of the Bulls and why he wouldn’t take it: “I don’t trust the ownership.” Verbatim.</p><p>Billy, don’t wait to see the majesty of their grand plan. You’ve already given six years of your coaching career to them. Any more benefits them, not you. What you’ve gotten in return so far is a stain impossible to remove from your HOF coaching career. A scarlet (blood) red “B.”</p><p>One that shouldn’t have any continuing years associated with it after 2026. Please let this be your “Year of the Exit.” Take to heart what Salt (of Salt-N-Pepa) said on-record the year after the people you are meeting with purchased this team: “My cow just died, I don’t need your Bull.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" entries.author: Scoop Jackson

entries.title: Percentage of Black MLB players increases in consecutive years for the 1st time in two decades
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — Major League Baseball said Friday the percentage of Black players on Opening Day rosters increased in consecutive years for the first time in at least two decades.</p><p>MLB said that 6.8% of players on Opening Day rosters, injured lists and the restricted list were Black, up from 6.2% at the start of the 2025 season and 6.0% at the beginning of 2024.</p><p>This year's 0.6% increase was the most in a season since a 0.7% rise from 2017 to 2018.</p><p>The Cubs, San Francisco Giants, Boston Red Sox, San Diego Padres and Pittsburgh Pirates are all without a single African-American player. The Padres also don’t have a Black player in the minors on their 40-man roster.</p><p>The Institute for Diversity and Ethics in Sport at Central Florida issued annual studies that showed the percentage was 18% when its reports started in 1991.</p><p>Twenty of the 64 Black players had been in programs such as the MLB Youth Academy, Breakthrough Series, DREAM Series, Nike RBI and the Hank Aaron Invitational.</p><p>MLB said the total includes 22 players 25 or younger and eight older than 32. The average age of Black players was 27.8 and the overall average 29.25.</p><p>In addition, 17 Black players assigned to the minor leagues were on opening day 40-man rosters, including seven from MLB development programs. That group included Milwaukee outfielder Blake Perkins, who was brought up to the Brewers on March 26.</p><p><b>Contributing:</b> <i>USA TODAY Sports</i></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Michelada Fest anuncia su programa completo y un día gratis extra tras las cancelaciones del año pasado
entries.summary: <p>Los organizadores del <a class="Link" href="https://www.michefest.live/" target="_blank" >Michelada Fest</a> anunciaron el jueves que el festival de fin de semana volverá a Chicago después de que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/05/07/cancelan-michelada-fest-2025" target="_blank" >el evento del año pasado fuera</a> cancelado debido a temores en torno al “clima político” y problemas con las visas para artistas extranjeros.</p><p>Michelada Fest, conocido simplemente como Miche Fest, celebra la música, el arte, la comida y la bebida latinas, especialmente el cóctel mexicano que da nombre al festival: la michelada.</p><p>El evento se llevará a cabo del 26 al 28 de junio en su nueva ubicación en Union Park y contará con una alineación que incluye a Latin Mafia, el rapero y cantante puertorriqueño Jhayco, la cantautora Jessie Reyez y el rapero y cantante jamaicano Sean Paul.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-840000" name="module-840000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Más de una docena de artistas locales y nacionales se presentarán, entre ellos el grupo mexicano Dareyes de la Sierra, el productor Ovy on the Drums y la cantante Paloma Mami. Varios artistas locales, como la DJ Eva Maria y Las Vibras de Chicago, también subirán al escenario ese fin de semana.</p><p>Los organizadores también están ampliando el festival a tres días este año. El primer día del festival, el viernes, con la participación de DannyLux y Herencia de Patrones, será gratuito.</p><p>Los fanáticos notarán que hay menos artistas internacionales en la programación de este año, y eso se hizo intencionalmente, dijo el fundador de Miche Fest, Fernando Nieto.</p><p>Los organizadores citaron problemas con las visas de artistas internacionales, así como el “rápidamente cambiante clima político”, entre las razones de la cancelación del año pasado.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3eaf4f" name="image-3eaf4f"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a6874a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e2c74c9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/407dfdb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El artista latino Dannylux se presentó ante una multitud de fanáticos fervientes en Lollapalooza en 2022." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/407dfdb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/34276e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/407dfdb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6083x3414+0+321/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2Fa0%2F8a4cc1324800b4589e7ceb1653cf%2Flolladay3-073122-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El artista latino Dannylux se presenta en Lollapalooza en 2022. El cantante y compositor es el cabeza del programa del primer día de Miche Fest.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kate Scott/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La aplicación de la ley de inmigración es otra preocupación para los organizadores. El 16 de septiembre, la administración de Trump comenzó su campaña de deportaciones con el Operativo Midway Blitz, lo que puso a Chicago y los suburbios en alerta máxima, especialmente en los vecindarios de mayoría latina e inmigrante.</p><p>“Es algo que está en nuestra mente constantemente”, comentó Nieto. “Queremos no solo proteger a nuestros [fanáticos] sino también a nuestro personal y a nuestros vendedores que podrían tener trabajadores indocumentados... Estamos muy orgullosos de dar estos pasos adicionales necesarios”.</p><p>Nieto agregó que los voluntarios y el personal de Miche Fest recibirán capacitación en tácticas de desescalada si surgen problemas entre la policía y artistas, vendedores o fanáticos.</p><p>“Sentimos que nuestra cultura estaba bajo ataque y que estaban apuntando no solo a la comunidad, sino también a los artistas y a la música en general”, dijo Nieto. “Tuvimos entre cuatro y cinco artistas que perdieron sus visas entre dos y tres semanas después de que cancelamos”.</p><p>Para el festival de este año, los organizadores de Miche Fest tomaron la decisión consciente de incluir a artistas cuyas visas están en regla.</p><p>“Desafortunadamente, esto limitó y cambió parte de nuestro enfoque, pero sentimos que tenemos una alineación realmente genial y fuerte”, continuó Nieto. “Fue el camino más seguro para construir la alineación del festival y evitar enfrentar ese problema este año”.</p><p>Después de que los organizadores cancelaron el evento del año pasado, se concentraron en construir una coalición sólida de alianzas para el relanzamiento.</p><p>Eso incluye trabajar con el artista visual de Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/murals-mosaics/2022/1/28/22894092/sentrock-murals-chicago-fulton-market-joseph-perez" target="_blank" >Sentrock</a>, quien está a cargo de las instalaciones de arte, y un “mundo de artistas” en el recinto del festival, dijo Nieto. El colectivo de DJs de Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/sentido.wav/" target="_blank" >Sentido</a>, está curando artistas locales para actuar en un segundo escenario añadido, y la marca de moda de Chicago y Durango, Gente Fina, está organizando una venta de mercancía exclusiva en el lugar.</p><p>El festival contará con 14 diferentes vendedores de michelada, así como con muchas opciones de comida local.</p><p>El festival de este año también tiene una nueva ubicación: Después de años de que los organizadores trabajaran para trasladarlo al lado oeste, Miche Fest ahora se llevará a cabo en Union Park, 1501 W. Randolph St. El festival se mudó a Oakwood Beach de Pilsen en 2024<a class="Link" href="https://southsideweekly.com/5th-pilsen-michelada-festival/" target="_blank" > después de que los residentes plantearan quejas</a> sobre la cantidad de tráfico de peatones, ruido y problemas de estacionamiento en el vecindario.</p><p>“Siendo transparentes, hemos estado tratando de entrar a Union Park durante los últimos años”, dijo Nieto. Logísticamente, comentó, operar el festival en Oakwood Beach era un poco un “dolor de cabeza”.</p><p>La proximidad de Union Park a las líneas verde y rosa del tren y varias rutas de autobús, además de la presencia de varios estacionamientos en el área, lo convierten en una ubicación ideal, afirmó Nieto.</p><p>“Nos hará la vida un millón de veces más fácil”, dijo. Si bien Miche Fest no tiene un acuerdo de varios años con Union Park, un portavoz dijo que tiene la intención de convertirlo en el hogar del festival por muchos años.</p><p>Para rendir homenaje a sus raíces orientadas a la comunidad, el primer día del festival será de entrada gratuita, dijo Nieto. En lugar de boletos, se alienta a los asistentes a realizar una donación sugerida de $10 a La Casa Norte, organización sin fines de lucro para los residentes de Chicago que enfrentan inseguridad de vivienda.</p><p>“Sabemos que no todos pueden permitirse asistir, así que esto es para ellos. Esto es para la comunidad, esto es para la accesibilidad y la inclusión”, dijo Nieto.</p><p>Los boletos ya están a la venta en <a class="Link" href="http://michefest.live" target="_blank" >MicheFest.live</a> y comienzan en $89 para el sábado y el domingo. Los niños menores de cinco años tienen entrada gratuita con un adulto que tenga boleto.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" >La Voz Chicago</a></p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Casi 70 grupos de Chicago reciben microbecas tras recortes de SNAP y campaña de inmigración
entries.summary: <p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson anunció el jueves que 67 organizaciones de Chicago recibirán microsubvenciones de $4,500, cada una a través del Greater Chicago Food Depository.</p><p>El dinero se está distribuyendo para ayudar a aliviar la presión que sienten los negocios pequeños y los grupos comunitarios de Chicago tras la campaña federal de deportaciones y los recortes a la asistencia alimentaria durante la administración de Trump.</p><p>“Tengo que admitir que a veces en el gobierno podemos movernos un poco lento”, dijo Johnson en el College Malcolm X el jueves. “Las subvenciones de $4,500 que se están otorgando, no sólo estamos invirtiendo en los servicios que ustedes brindan, sino en el liderazgo que ustedes ofrecen y la confianza que han construido con los residentes. Ustedes encarnan el espíritu de nuestra responsabilidad colectiva”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d60000" name="module-d60000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>En noviembre de 2025, Johnson firmó una orden ejecutiva que asignó los recursos de la Municipalidad al Food Depository para apoyar a las organizaciones y negocios locales afectadas por una pausa en el financiamiento del Programa de Asistencia Nutricional Suplementaria (SNAP) durante el cierre del gobierno de 43 días.</p><p>Como parte de ese esfuerzo, la Municipalidad lanzó el programa de subvenciones para ayudar a los negocios afectados tanto por la reducción de poder de compra de SNAP como por la disminución del tráfico peatonal debido a la aplicación de la ley de inmigración federal.</p><p>La pausa en la financiación de SNAP se sintió para muchos habitantes de Chicago a principios de noviembre, pero los negocios pequeños y organizaciones se vieron afectados meses antes debido al aumento de los operativos de inmigración. Además, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/16/adultos-mayores-de-chicago-en-alto-riesgo-de-perder-asistencia-alimentaria-de-snap-con-nuevos-requisitos-laborales" target="_blank" >los nuevos requisitos laborales ampliados</a> para los beneficiarios de SNAP que entraron en vigor esta primavera se espera que saquen a aproximadamente 150,000 personas del programa a partir de mayo, según las últimas estimaciones de funcionarios estatales.</p><p>El organizador principal de Working Family Solidarity, Kevin Johnson Jr., de 26 años, dijo que más de la mitad de su organización es hispana y que cerca del 40% de sus miembros son indocumentados.</p><p>“Directamente después de que se recortaron los beneficios de SNAP, se convirtió en una especie de Armagedón para nosotros un poco”, dijo.</p><p>El grupo comunitario, con sede en Pilsen, brinda apoyo a las comunidades afroamericanas y latinas de Chicago.</p><p>“Una de nuestras trabajadoras, que es madre soltera de dos hijos, tuvo sus beneficios recortados, así que esto es algo que literalmente está impidiendo que las personas puedan alimentar a sus hijos y alimentarse a sí mismas”, dijo Johnson.</p><div Benton/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La organización tiene la intención de utilizar su subvención para realizar colectas de alimentos y otros recursos en todo Chicago.</p><p>Dulce La granja, ubicada en el vecindario de Englewood, recibía beneficios de SNAP para cubrir costos operativos.</p><p>“Todavía estamos en producción”, dijo Morales, “pero ha impactado los ingresos que nos llegan”.</p><p>La granja familiar tiene un camión que utiliza para hacer entregas a mercados locales en todo Chicago.</p><p>“Queremos seguir alimentando a las personas, pero es muy difícil cuando class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El Greater Chicago Food Depository lanzó el programa de microsubvenciones de ayuda mutua.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Somer Van Benton/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Morales mencionó que la subvención de $4,500 ayudará a compensar el aumento de los precios de la gasolina y los costos del seguro para el camión.</p><p>Maria’h Foster, del grupo Life is Work, indicó que la inseguridad alimentaria en el vecindario de Austin siempre ha sido prevalente, pero los recortes en el financiamiento de SNAP han amplificado la necesidad de más recursos.</p><p>Foster comentó que la organización tiene la intención de utilizar su subvención para contribuir a su despensa de alimentos existente, enfatizando que hay una amplia gama de comunidades en situación de necesidad.</p><p>“No son solo las comunidades afroamericanas y latinas las que se están viendo afectadas”, dijo Foster. “Las poblaciones envejecidas, los ancianos, las personas con discapacidad, las personas con VIH, los veteranos —esas son las poblaciones que también han sido vulnerables y afectadas por esto que, en cierta manera, son dejadas de lado”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Somer Van Benton | WBEZ

entries.title: Cool temperatures on Saturday expected to be pushed out by Sunday’s warm, windy weather
entries.summary: <p>Chicagoans will need to gear up for varying weather throughout the weekend, as cooler temperatures on Saturday give way to a warm, windy Sunday, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>Temperatures will likely remain relatively low on Saturday, with a high of 47 degrees downtown. Those in the suburbs may enjoy a bit more warmth with temperatures reaching high 50s and low 60s. Though weather is expected to stay mild throughout the day, some showers may pass by in the evening and through the night.</p><p>Sunday will bring a twist. Temperatures will reach the low 70s near the lakefront and close to 80 degrees southwest of the metro area, but high temperatures will be dampened by winds gusting as high as around 40 mph and storms possibly rolling during the evening through the night.</p><p>Meteorologist Kevin Doom said Sunday is probably not a day for enjoying shore, given the high winds and low temperatures of the lake.</p><p>“Everybody has a different taste, but in my opinion, I wouldn't want to be at the beach," Doom said.</p><p>Doom added he does not expect the high winds to significantly affect travel times at O’Hare International Airport.</p><p>“Given the direction and the wind speeds, the airport should be able to mitigate those impacts pretty well, but it's certainly possible.” Doom said. “Maybe they can slow down the arrival or departure rate a little bit.”</p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Shota Imanaga doesn't allow any hits over six innings, but Cubs still lose 2-0
entries.summary: <p>Shota Imanaga gave the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> six innings of no-hit pitching Friday. The Cubs, despite the return of slugger Seiya Suzuki to the middle of their lineup, gave themselves no hits when even one might have made the difference between winning and losing.</p><p>The result was predictable. Despite what ranked among Imanaga’s best starts as a Cub — six hitless innings, one walk, and just one batter faced over the minimum — the Cubs were beaten, 2-0, by the Pittsburgh Pirates on a desultory afternoon in Wrigley Field overstuffed with missed opportunities.</p><p>Twice the Cubs loaded the bases and failed to score. Twice they left two runners on. They had six hits, all singles. They drew seven walks. They put the leadoff man on base four times.</p><p>And they came up empty each time, combining to go 0 for 8 with runners in scoring position which gave a crowd of 28,811 little to cheer about.</p><p>“It’s one of those offensive days,’’ manager Craig Counsell said, “where you do a lot of things right and then you walk away and you’re just, ‘How did we not score today? How did we have a zero on the board?' And so, it’s frustrating.’’</p><p>It was a particularly galling day for Pete-Crow Armstrong, who hit into a double play in the second, flied out with two on in the fourth, struck out with two on in the sixth, and avoided hitting into another double play in the eighth only because shortstop Konnor Griffin’s relay was wide.</p><p>Imanaga had thrown 100 pitches through six innings, so there was no thought by Counsell to have him continue. “Easy decision,’’ he said.</p><p>His best outing as a Cub? Imanaga wasn’t prepared to call it that.</p><p>“I can’t recall every single start that I’ve made,’’ he said, “but thinking back, I can’t say for certain this was the best.’’</p><p>This was the eighth time in his 57 starts as a Cub that the left-hander has pitched at least six innings without allowing a run.</p><p>“I think Shota has really had three good starts, minus one pitch,’’ Counsell said, alluding to the three-run home run Imanaga gave up to the Nationals’ Joey Wiemer in his first start.</p><p>“And that’s great news for us.’’</p><p>On Sept. 4, 2024, Shota threw seven no-hit innings against the Pirates in what would become a combined no-hitter in a 12-0 win at Wrigley. The Pirates had just three holdovers in Friday’s lineup from that team — Bryan Reynolds, Nick Gonzales and Oneil Cruz — and it was Reynolds who combined with newcomer Ryan O’Hearn to make sure history would not repeat itself. O’Hearn hit reliever Caleb Thielbar’s third pitch into right field for a single. Reynolds hit Thielbar’s next pitch, a hanging curveball, into the left-field bleachers.</p><p>Thielbar was not in the Cubs’ clubhouse, which was bereft of both bodies and music after this loss. Alex Bregman was deep in conversation with a staff member in front of his locker. Carson Kelly, who was on base four times — a single and three walks — talked about Shota’s start — and Suzuki discussed his return from his hamstring injury.</p><p>Otherwise, crickets. When a club has just been shut out a second time and three regulars are nearly 50 at-bats into a season and are all hitting under .200 ( Bregman, Michael Busch and Dansby Swanson, the low man at .140), having to answer questions can be a painful exercise.</p><p>Jed Hoyer, chatting with reporters before the game, said taking the temperature of a team this early in the season is an unproductive exercise. He likened it to an NFL team trying to draw conclusions after one game or the equivalent of playing the first hole in a round of golf.</p><p>“Our offense projects to be an excellent offense,’’ he said. “We’re [6 and 7] and we haven’t really hit yet. I think that actually excites me, because we have really good players that haven’t gotten going yet, and at the end of the day, they’ll get to where their baseball card says they should be.’’</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Why Blackhawks' young defense reminds Duncan Keith of his early years
entries.summary: <p>In retrospect, young <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> defenseman Wyatt Kaiser's performance against the Ducks in March 2025 marked a turning point in his career.</p><p>His dominant showing ended Kaiser's nearly two years of bouncing between the NHL and AHL and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/11/05/blackhawks-wyatt-kaiser-defensive-stalwart-duncan-keith-nhl" target="_blank" >established him</a> as an important piece of the defensive corps the Hawks are assembling.</p><p>It turns out another important person happened to be watching that night: Legendary Hawks defenseman Duncan Keith.</p><p>Keith said Friday he isn't able to watch as many Hawks games as he would like, since they're often finished by the time he gets home from coaching his son Colton's youth-hockey team in British Columbia. </p><p>But he still follows the Hawks closely, and when they're on the West Coast, he is often able to watch.</p><p>That particular Hawks-Ducks game fell in that category. Keith took notice of Kaiser's quick footwork to dart around two Ducks at the blue line, skate into space and snipe the game's opening goal. It reminded him vaguely of himself.</p><p>"I got to meet [Kaiser] when we were here in January," Keith said. "He looked bigger on the ice, so I hope I looked that big when I was playing. He moves really good. He's a good-skating defenseman."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe class="brightcove-embed" src="https://players.brightcove.net/pages/v1/index.html?accountId=6415718365001&playerId=D3UCGynRWU&videoId=6369515813112&mode=iframe" width="640" height="360" scrolling="no" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>In fact, this entire Hawks defense vaguely reminds Keith of the crew he joined in his first NHL seasons. That only came after he spent two full seasons in the AHL, which is another fact worth remembering when judging prospects.</p><p>Keith is obviously remembered for his enormous contributions to the Hawks' dynasty era, which earned him <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/06/24/blackhawks-duncan-keith-hockey-hall-of-fame-nhl" target="_blank" >induction</a> into the Hockey Hall of Fame in November and now induction into the new Blackhawks Hall of Fame. Keith, Steve Larmer, Troy Murray and the nine Hawks with already retired numbers were honored privately Friday and will be honored again Saturday at the United Center.</p><p>But it's easy to forget the Hawks went 26-43-13 in 2005-06 — Keith and Brent Seabrook's rookie year — and 31-42-9 in 2006-07. Those teams struggled mightily.</p><p>That firsthand experience gives Keith optimism that this ultra-young Hawks team will eventually find its way and learn how to succeed. It also gives him patience when analyzing the process from afar.</p><p>"They've got a great young group of defensemen," Keith said. "You look at the size of some of these guys and the way they can skate and how young they are. [Artyom] Levshunov is, what, 20 years old?</p><p>"It's going to take a few years to get that experience and that internal competition to push one another for each other for ice time. I see a lot of similarities with our young group that we had when I started out on this team.</p><p>"Everyone pushed one another to become better, and slowly you're able to add some players. We added [Marian Hossa]. Hopefully they get to a point where they can, here in Chicago, add a piece like that."</p><p>That last comment is one that general manager Kyle Davidson should notice. It's a sentiment being brought up more and more by relevant people, and it will be up to him to make happen this summer.</p><p>The rebuild's fate will ultimately be decided by how this existing core pans out, though, and Keith knows that well. He witnessed the same team that won 26 games in 2005-06 end up winning 52 games and a Stanley Cup four years later.</p><p>"It was just [about] the work ethic every day to get better and improve," he said. "Before you knew it, we started adding more players, the players that we had here kept getting better and the snowball got bigger and bigger. It's just [about] staying focused on getting better and improving.</p><p>"Certainly, Connor [Bedard] has shown improvement in his game and that drive and motivation [to] be a good leader for this group. But it's going to take everyone to step up."</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Royal feud? White Sox starter Anthony Kay miffed with ‘chirping’ division rivals
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Have the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> got some beef on the barbecue in Kansas City?</p><p>Starter <b>Anthony Kay</b> sure thinks so.</p><p>“Those guys like to chirp a little bit, it seems like,” Kay said after staring down Royals outfielder <b>Jac Caglianone</b>, who initiated some cold, hard eye contact on the way to first base after taking a 94 mph pitch high and inside during the Sox’ 2-0 win Thursday.</p><p>“I don’t think we’re going to take that anymore,” Kay declared after touching 98 mph and looking sharp in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/09/white-sox-royals-kauffman-stadium-anthony-kay-noah-schultz" >his first MLB win since 2021</a>. “We’re going to go right at them.”</p><p>Those might sound like fighting words that could heat up their American League Central rivalry — but Caglianone downplayed the potential for any divisional dustup.</p><p>“No malice or ill intent behind it,” Caglianone said in the home clubhouse at Kauffman Stadium the day after his mean-mugging.</p><p>The official scorer ruled the 23-year-old was plunked by Kay’s sinker, “but I didn't get hit.” He would’ve gotten a free pass either way on the 3-1 pitch well out of the zone.</p><p>“We weren’t swinging it well. … I wasn't sitting there thinking that he was doing it on purpose,” Caglianone said of Kay, who hit Royals catcher <b>Salvador Perez</b> with an 86 mph sweeper in the first inning. “There was a guy on first. I was just moreso trying to be like a little spark plug, see if I could get the guys fired up.”</p><p>Caglianone, a World Baseball Classic teammate of the Sox’ <b>Kyle Teel</b> and <b>Sam Antonacci</b> for Italy, shrugged off Kay’s pointed reaction.</p><p>“I saw what he said last night, and I was just like, it's not that deep,” said Caglianone, who struck out in his first two at-bats against the lefty. “Frustrated? I mean, yeah, obviously, he got me twice. So you know, not happy with myself.”</p><p>The Royals’ 2024 first-round draft pick said there’s “nothing out of the baseball norm” in their rivalry, which has been one-sided since before Caglianone’s debut last season. Entering Friday’s game, KC had feasted on the Sox to a 24-5 record since September 2023.</p><p>And Caglianone’s not looking for any feud in the future.</p><p>“They’ve got a lot of firepower in that bullpen. Wouldn’t want to take on one the ribs from <b>Grant Taylor</b>,” he laughed.</p><p>Manager <b>Will Venable</b> agreed there was no new bad blood entering his second year on the job.</p><p>“It looked like some of the guys had a problem with Caglianone getting hit yesterday, which I can assure you was not intentional,” said Venable, who still liked seeing some fire from his pitcher.</p><p>“He takes pride in being on this team. He’s going to have all the guys’ backs,” Venable said. “I love that response.”</p><h3><b>Pope hats for all</b></h3><p>Sox fans hoping to get their hands on a South Side-branded pope hat had their prayers answered Friday as the team announced they’re <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/08/white-sox-pope-leo-pope-themed-hats-reds" target="_blank" ><u>expanding the papal promo</u></a> for all ticketholders to the Aug. 11 home game versus the Reds due to “overwhelming” demand.</p><p>“The fans have spoken, and unlike some of our more limited quantity promotions, the White Sox Pope Hat is one we believe all fans should have the opportunity to take home,” the Sox’ chief marketing officer, Brooks Boyer, said in a statement.</p><p>The Sox’ most famous fan, Dolton native <b>Pope Leo XIV</b>, probably won’t be in attendance, but he’s got <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/11/19/justin-ishbia-pope-white-sox-stadium?utm_campaign=mrf-twitter-Suntimes&mrfcid=20251119691e2ae87f66774cdbd2a9b8" target="_blank" ><u>an open invitation</u></a> to Rate Field.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ba0000" name="image-ba0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
