entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Old-school gets it done for Fenwick's Jake Thies
entries.summary: <p>From the transfer portal to big NIL deals to coaches job-hopping, the college football world constantly is evolving.</p><p>But there’s one place where stability reigns, and that was a big draw for Fenwick’s Jake Thies.</p><p>One of the heroes of the Friars’ stunning Class 6A championship victory against East St. Louis last fall, Thies recently committed to Iowa in the 2027 recruiting cycle.</p><p>The two-way standout had some good options from the Big Ten (Illinois, Northwestern, Purdue, UCLA) to the SEC (Vanderbilt). But Thies liked what he saw in the Hawkeyes, a perennially successful program run by Kirk Ferentz, the longest-tenured coach in major-college football.</p><p>‘‘Right now, the way college football is changing, Iowa hasn’t bought into any of that,’’ Thies told the Sun-Times. ‘‘They’re staying true to what they do — how they recruit, how they handle stuff within their team.</p><p>‘‘It’s an old-school team, a physical team.’’</p><p>If that sounds like Fenwick, which has won two state titles in the last four seasons under coach Matt Battaglia, it’s no coincidence.</p><p>‘‘That was another thing I was looking for,’’ Thies said of a team with a similar mindset. ‘‘I’ve enjoyed my time at Fenwick. The program fits me super-well.’’</p><p>The 6-foot, 185-pound Thies has been a force on both sides of the ball for the Friars at safety and running back. In the victory against East St. Louis, he piled up 200 all-purpose yards, 19 tackles, an interception and two touchdowns — one on a 74-yard run.</p><p>Thies, the consensus No. 23 prospect in Illinois in the 2027 cycle, has a college future as a defensive back. Iowa’s reputation on that side of the ball and its track record of making players better factored heavily into his decision.</p><p>‘‘That’s the place that’s going to develop me the best,’’ Thies said. ‘‘The DBs coach [Phil Parker] is also the defensive coordinator. No school is going to be able to top that.’’</p><p>Thies started playing flag football when he was 3 and tackle when he was 6, so it’s no surprise he comes from a football family. Dad Scott, who is Fenwick’s athletic director, played at DePauw, and older brother Tommy, also a mainstay on the 2025 state champs, is heading to Miami (Ohio).</p><p>Thies said he is excited to move on to the next level, playing in front of huge crowds on national broadcasts.</p><p>Before that, however, he’s all-in for his final season at Fenwick.</p><p>‘‘Right now, I’m just really focusing on being bigger, stronger, faster every season,’’ Thies said. ‘‘I’ve been working with Pete Houlihan, a DB trainer, working on the small details. If you’re a step late, that’s too much.’’</p><p>A point of emphasis is making sure the Friars have the right mindset this fall.</p><p>‘‘The main message I’m trying to get across to our guys [is] we can’t live off a win from last season,’’ Thies said. ‘‘It’s not the same team. Not getting complacent is huge.’’<br></p>
entries.author: Mike Clark

entries.title: Pritzker says DHS threat to deny customs-processing resources in sanctuary cities would hurt Chicago economy
entries.summary: <p>Gov. JB Pritzker strongly pushed back against Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin’s suggestion this week that his depatment might not provide customs-processing resources to sanctuary cities like Chicago.</p><p>“We ought to continue to demand that the Republicans fund TSA and fund the things that we need in order to keep the economy running,” Pritzker said Friday at an unrelated news conference. “If they take this away, they’re going to hurt the economy even more.”</p><p>Appearing Monday on Fox News, Mullin questioned whether cities with sanctuary rules — which prohibit city workers from cooperating with federal immigration agencies — should be “processing customs into their city.” Federal agencies under DHS handle customs processing for travelers.</p><p>“If they’re a sanctuary city and are receiving international flights and we’re asking them to partner with us at the airport, but once they walk out of the airport they’re not going to enforce immigration policy — maybe we need to have a really hard look at that because we need to focus on cities that want to work with us,” Mullin said.</p><p>Mullin’s comments came as DHS is under its longest shutdown over a dispute about funding for the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency. The Transportation Security Administration was caught up in the budget impasse for weeks, with workers calling out, which has led to long security lines at major airports. Pay for TSA workers has since been reinstated despite the ongoing DHS shutdown.</p><p>When asked by Fox News host Bret Baier if Mullin’s comments meant he would withhold customs-processing funding from sanctuary cities, Mullin said, “We’re going to have to start prioritizing things at some point.”</p><p>“I’m going to have to be forced to make hard decisions,” Mullin said. “Who’s willing to work with us and partner with us?”</p><p>The Trump administration has singled out Chicago and other Democratic-led cities for their sanctuary policies. Chicago was among several cities targeted last year in Trump’s aggressive deportation campaign.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: State revenues up but uncertainty looms as fiscal year closes out
entries.summary: <p>State government revenues were up $1.571 billion at the end of the third quarter, according to the most recent report from the legislature’s bipartisan, bicameral Commission on Government Forecasting and Accountability.</p><p>The almost $1.6 billion in new revenues is an increase of 4.2% The state budget was crafted last year with an expectation that revenues would rise just 2.3% for the full fiscal year, so that’s pretty darned good news.</p><p>The commission revised its projected revenue estimates upward last month by $684 million, or 1.2%, above the original budget forecast last May. The continued revenue increases in March “further reinforced” last month’s upward revision, the commission claimed in its latest revenue report issued at the beginning of April.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-080000" name="module-080000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>So why is the newly revised annual revenue estimate less than half of the increase so far this fiscal year?</p><p>Well, as the commission notes, April can be a volatile month. It’s tough to accurately predict what will happen when people make their tax payments.</p><p>Also, the state has a history of not accurately estimating how personal and corporate income taxes as well as the Personal Property Replacement Tax will break out during the tax year. A “true-up” has to be done, and this year’s state budget expected that state coffers would take the short end of the stick and local governments would benefit via the Personal Property Replacement Tax, but that hasn’t yet happened. So, the commission is wary.</p><p>Also, without further changes to state corporate tax laws to decouple from last summer’s massive federal tax cuts, the huge slide in corporate tax receipts will continue. Many of our state corporate taxes are tied to federal laws.</p><p>As of now, those corporate receipts are down 6.2% for the year, but they were projected to increase by 10.8% when the state budget was passed before Congress slashed so many corporate taxes.</p><p>Only mentioned briefly in the latest monthly report is the potential economic fallout from the attack on Iran and the subsequent closure of a key petroleum shipping lane. It’s such a volatile situation that nobody really knows what to expect in the fiscal year’s final three months.</p><p>And that brings us to President Donald Trump’s recently proposed budget for Fiscal Year 2027, which starts Oct. 1.</p><p>The proposal would slash funds for infrastructure projects by $15.2 billion, reduce public school funding by $8.5 billion, eliminate the program to help low-income households with utility bills, cut higher education funding by $2.7 billion, reduce law enforcement and public safety programs by $1.7 billion, cut homelessness grants by $393 million, eliminate the school meals program, etc.</p><p>That’s on top of the gargantuan cuts to Medicaid and nutrition assistance programs included in last summer’s reconciliation budget.</p><p>Some or even most of this proposed budget appears to be performative. The president tried to get rid of the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program last year, for example. But Trump is convinced that social programs are riddled with corruption, and he said before unveiling his budget proposal that childcare, Medicaid and even the federal Medicare program should all be state funded.</p><p>“The United States can't take care of day care,” the president said at a private event.</p><p>“That has to be up to a state. We can't take care of day care. We're a big country. We have 50 states. We have all these other people we’re fighting wars with. We can't take care of daycare. You’ve got to let a state take care of daycare. And they should pay for it, too. They should pay. They have to raise their taxes, but they should pay for it. … Medicaid, Medicare, all these individual things. They can do it on a state basis. You can't do it on a federal. We have to take care of one thing, military protection. We have to guard the country. But all these little things, all these little scams that have taken place, you have to let states take care of them.”</p><p>Trump also mentioned that education funding should be a state funding issue.</p><p><i>Rich Miller also publishes Capitol Fax, a daily political newsletter, and CapitolFax.com.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-180000" name="module-180000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Rich Miller

entries.title: Familias refugiadas son el grupo más reciente que enfrenta el recorte de los beneficios alimentarios de SNAP
entries.summary: <p>K.Q. compartía una habitación en un hotel con su esposo y cuatro hijos cuando recibió una tarjeta con fondos del Programa de Asistencia Nutricional Suplementaria (SNAP)</p><p>La familia acababa de llegar a Chicago, una ciudad donde no conocían a nadie, después de ser aceptados en el programa de reasentamiento de refugiados de Estados Unidos. Venían del Líbano, donde pasaron 12 años tras huir de la guerra civil mortal en Siria.</p><p>Llevó a sus cuatro hijos a la tienda de comida, diciéndoles que podían comprar cualquier cosa que quisieran con la asistencia alimentaria federal. Era un placer especial para una familia que en Líbano luchaba por llegar a fin de mes.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6e0000" name="module-6e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Estamos tan felices porque puedo ir a comprar todo para mis hijos”, dijo K.Q., quien pidió no ser identificada por completo por temor a que pudiera perjudicar su solicitud para convertirse en residente permanente legal. “En Líbano tuvimos [una] época difícil y no tenemos suficiente dinero para comprar nada que los niños necesiten”.</p><p>Pero, tras menos de tres años aquí, están a punto de perder la ayuda alimentaria, junto con miles de otros refugiados, asilados y sobrevivientes de la trata de personas.</p><p>La extensa reforma fiscal del presidente Donald Trump, aprobada el año pasado, limita a qué inmigrantes pueden acceder al programa de alimentos SNAP, junto con <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/16/adultos-mayores-de-chicago-en-alto-riesgo-de-perder-asistencia-alimentaria-de-snap-con-nuevos-requisitos-laborales" target="_blank" >la implementación de nuevos requisitos laborales</a> para otros.</p><p>Los cambios para los inmigrantes entraron en vigor en Illinois el 1 de abril y forman parte de restricciones más amplias al programa de reasentamiento de refugiados.</p><p>Hasta 16,000 personas podrían perder los beneficios de SNAP debido al cambio en <a class="Link" href="https://www.dhs.state.il.us/?item=177780" target="_blank" >la elegibilidad para inmigrantes</a>, según el Departamento de Servicios Humanos de Illinois (IDHS)</p><p>Los cambios en las políticas de Trump han puesto a los refugiados en un dilema, dicen las agencias de reasentamiento. Sólo pueden recibir beneficios de SNAP una vez que se convierten en residentes permanentes legales. Pero el gobierno federal no está procesando sus solicitudes de residencia permanente.</p><p>Históricamente, se les ha otorgado a los refugiados un estatus migratorio con un camino hacia la ciudadanía.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e44b21" name="image-e44b21"></a> <picture data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Una foto de una refugiada siria llamada K.Q." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51cd350/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>K.Q. había esperado obtener su diploma de GED antes de intentar encontrar un trabajo, pero dice que ahora probablemente tendrá que buscar trabajo para compensar la pérdida esperada de los beneficios de SNAP para su familia.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Para K.Q., este cambio la ha dejado confundida y sin saber cómo alimentará a su familia.</p><p>“Vinimos aquí porque nos eligieron para venir”, dijo. “¿Por qué ahora no nos ayudan a vivir aquí? Porque aquí es caro”.</p><h3>Nube de incertidumbre para los refugiados</h3><p>Durante décadas en Estados Unidos, cuando los refugiados llegaban por primera vez, podían acceder a SNAP y otros programas de seguridad social, como el seguro de salud Medicaid. Ese apoyo fue fundamental, dicen las personas de reasentamiento de refugiados, para proporcionar estabilidad, ya que los primeros trabajos de los refugiados suelen pagar el salario mínimo. La mayor parte de sus ingresos se destina a la renta y otros gastos básicos.</p><p>“Los refugiados han contado con esta parte vital de la red de seguridad para ayudarlos mientras intentan empezar de nuevo aquí en Chicago”, dijo Sally Schulze, gerente de comunicaciones de RefugeeOne, organización sin fines de lucro de reasentamiento en Chicago.</p><p>Ese fue el caso de K.Q. y su familia, quienes vivieron en una habitación de hotel durante dos meses antes de mudarse a un apartamento en Chicago. La familia recibió inicialmente alrededor de $1,100 en beneficios mensuales de SNAP, que luego se redujeron a $635 una vez que su esposo comenzó a trabajar en un aeropuerto local. Él es el único sostén de la familia, mientras que K.Q. se ha enfocado en mejorar su inglés mediante clases con un grupo comunitario.</p><p>La pérdida de SNAP es sólo un desafío que enfrentan los refugiados. La administración de Trump redujo el número de refugiados permitidos a 7,500 para el actual año fiscal, la cifra más baja desde que comenzó el programa en 1980, <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2025/10/30/g-s1-95823/trump-administration-sets-lowest-ever-cap-on-refugee-admissions" target="_blank" >informó anteriormente NPR</a>. Eso es una disminución de 125,000 en 2025 bajo el presidente Joe Biden.</p><p>También persisten los temores en la comunidad de ser levantados en redadas y terminar en un centro de detención de inmigrantes, dicen los defensores.</p><p>World Relief Chicagoland, otra agencia local de reasentamiento, ha estado reuniéndose con familias para anticipar planes del gobierno para volver a entrevistarlas, esencialmente sirviendo como una segunda verificación de su estatus de refugiado, dijo Susan Sperry, directora ejecutiva de World Relief Chicagoland.</p><p>Y en noviembre pasado, los Servicios de Ciudadanía e Inmigración de Estados Unidos (USCIS) en un memorando interno dijeron que ya no estaban procesando solicitudes de refugiados para residencias permanentes, según la Asociación Estadounidense de Abogados de Inmigración. Generalmente, tras solicitar, tardaba alrededor de 13 meses recibir una residencia permanente.</p><p>En un comunicado, USCIS confirmó que pausó las decisiones para inmigrantes de países considerados de alto riesgo por la administración de Trump. No dijo cuándo terminaría la pausa.</p><p>“La seguridad nacional y la protección de cada estadounidense deben tomar prioridad, incluso cuando se comparan frente a desafíos potenciales como la escasez de mano de obra”, declaró la agencia en un correo electrónico, añadiendo que cree que la administración de Biden no examinó adecuadamente a los inmigrantes.</p><p>Siria, de donde K.Q. y su familia huyeron, figura entre los países considerados de alto riesgo porque aún se recupera de disturbios civiles y “carece de una autoridad central adecuada para emitir pasaportes o documentos civiles”, según la Casa Blanca.</p><p>La familia solicitó la residencia permanente legal hace aproximadamente un año pero, al igual que otros, no ha recibido actualizaciones sobre su solicitud.</p><p>“Espero que todo pueda cambiar porque también aquí necesitamos estar seguros”, comentó K.Q. “[Si] tenemos residencia permanente, podemos quedarnos aquí. Me preocupa, sí, por mis hijos, por mí”.</p><h3>‘Las matemáticas simplemente no están cuadrando’</h3><p>RefugeeOne anticipa que 175 familias, o 850 individuos a quienes sirven, perderán beneficios de SNAP en algún momento de este año, la mayoría de ellos niños, dijo Schulze. World Relief Chicagoland anticipa que más de 300 de sus familias perderán beneficios.</p><p>Los cambios en las solicitudes de la residencia permanente añaden incertidumbre.</p><p>“Sin la residencia permanente, eso significa que no podrán obtener esta asistencia alimentaria que tan desesperadamente necesitan durante meses, posiblemente incluso años, a menos que los legisladores hagan algunos cambios”, dijo Schulze.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ae17aa" name="image-ae17aa"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" La agencia de reasentamiento anticipa que 850 de las personas a las que atiende, la mayoría de ellas niños, pierdan la asistencia alimentaria a partir de este mes." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c5c018/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/62d802a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c5c018/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sally Schulze se sienta en la oficina principal de RefugeeOne en el vecindario de West Ridge. La agencia de reasentamiento anticipa que 850 de las personas a las que atiende, la mayoría de ellas niños, pierdan la asistencia alimentaria a partir de este mes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Comentó que su organización escucha de familias que esperan que sus beneficios terminen este mes.</p><p>World Relief Chicagoland está tratando de ayudar a los refugiados a encontrar trabajos adicionales o a aumentar sus ingresos, dijo Sperry. RefugeeOne también se está reuniendo con familias para revisar sus presupuestos familiares y ver qué, si acaso, podría eliminarse.</p><p>“Las matemáticas simplemente no están cuadrando para muchas de estas familias”, dijo Schulze. “Porque, especialmente para quienes tienen niños, simplemente no puedes llegar a fin de mes sin SNAP”.</p><p>K.Q. está calculando los números para su familia, pero es difícil ver cómo compensarán los $635 que perderán en SNAP. Lo usa para comprar pan, aceite para cocinar y carne Halal.</p><p>Jala un carrito de compras negro a sus clases de inglés, donde también hay una despensa de comida semanal. Durante una reciente visita, tomó dos galones de leche, pasta de lasaña, huevos y una piña para complementar las compras de su familia. La familia normalmente se acaba los beneficios de SNAP antes de que termine el mes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-378fd8" name="image-378fd8"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5cbff1b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7daae82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>K.Q. abastece una despensa de alimentos local para complementar lo que puede pagar con sus beneficios de SNAP.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>K.Q. veía las clases de idiomas como una forma de obtener un trabajo mejor remunerado algún día. Pero ahora piensa que probablemente tendrá que buscar un trabajo antes, aunque tomaba las clases para prepararse para el examen de GED.</p><p>“No sé qué podemos hacer, pero me preocupa eso”, dijo.</p><p>En otras familias de refugiados, los jóvenes adultos en la escuela tendrán que dejar la escuela para buscar trabajo a tiempo completo para ayudar a alimentar a la familia, dijo Schulze.</p><p>A nivel estatal, el Greater Chicago Food Depository y el Latino Policy Forum están abogando por un proyecto de ley que proporcione asistencia en efectivo a inmigrantes, como los refugiados, que perderán SNAP. La medida está pendiente en la Legislatura estatal.</p><p>Ambos grupos de reasentamiento también están refiriendo familias a despensas y colectando tarjetas de regalo de supermercados para ayudar a las familias.</p><p>“Esto no es sostenible”, dijo Schulze. “Simplemente no podemos reemplazar un programa del gobierno federal colectando tarjetas de regalo”.</p><h3>Soñando con un hijo que va a la universidad</h3><p>El esposo de K.Q. ahora pinta muebles para ganarse la vida, ganando lo justo para sobrevivir. Sus tres hijos menores asisten a la escuela y K.Q. se encarga de ellos. Aunque su renta ha aumentado, no quieren arriesgarse a mudarse y perderse cartas importantes sobre su caso de inmigración.</p><p>“Hacemos cualquier cosa que el gobierno nos dice”, dijo.</p><p>No sabía nada sobre Chicago antes de vivir aquí, pero sí sabía que necesitaba sacar a su familia del Líbano, ya que Siria seguía inestable. Su hijo mayor tuvo que dejar la escuela en Líbano para trabajar y complementar los ingresos de su esposo. K.Q. dijo que los niños sirios solo recibían aproximadamente cuatro horas de clases con materias limitadas.</p><p>En Chicago, ha visto a sus hijos prosperar en la escuela. Han aprendido inglés y están haciendo amigos. Uno de sus hijos, un estudiante tercer año de secundaria, sueña con ir a la universidad para estudiar computación.</p><p>“Está muy emocionado por eso y yo estoy tan feliz”, dijo K.Q. iluminándose. “Él me preguntó antes, ‘Mamá, si obtengo una beca para una universidad que esté lejos, ¿te molesta?’ Le dije, ‘No, este es tu futuro, pero te extrañaré’”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Trump administration wants to cut Illinois out of Great Lakes carp plan
entries.summary: <p><i>This coverage is made possible through a partnership between WBEZ and </i><a class="Link" href="http://grist.org/" target="_blank" ><i>Grist</i></a><i>, a nonprofit environmental media organization.</i></p><p>The Trump administration announced plans to put Michigan officials in charge of a $1.15 billion Chicago-area river barrier designed to keep invasive carp from reaching the Great Lakes.</p><p>In a <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/ASACivilWorks/status/2042298131896025544" target="_blank" ><u>statement posted Thursday</u></a> on X, Assistant Secretary of the Army for Civil Works Adam Telle said the agency was “aggressively moving” on the Brandon Road Interbasin Project near Joliet. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers added in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.usace.army.mil/Media/News-Releases/News-Release-Article-View/Article/4455556/us-army-corps-of-engineers-to-continue-work-on-brandon-road-interbasin-project/" target="_blank" >news release</a> that management would move from its Rock Island district to its Detroit district.</p><p>“President Trump has always been a champion of keeping invasive Asian carp out of the Great Lakes,” wrote Telle. “Our partners in the Great Lake states can’t allow one state to have undue influence and use it to play more games.”</p><p>Telle also accused Illinois of being an “unreliable partner, delinquent on its payments and real estate commitments.”</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker<a class="Link" href="https://x.com/GovPritzker/status/2042371859908370818?s=20" target="_blank" > responded to the social media post</a>, saying that “Illinois has upheld our commitments. Trump must stop this political stunt and start releasing the funds, get the project moving again, and protect the Great Lakes.” He warned that Illinois was <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/GovPritzker/status/2042371862731137225" target="_blank" >prepared to take the issue to the court</a>, adding that “Illinois owns the land the Brandon Road Project will be built on — Trump cannot just decide to give it away.”</p><p>Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer has worked with Illinois and the federal government to get the Brandon Road project “moving forward with urgency,” according to a statement from her press secretary Stacey LaRouche.</p><p>“Gov. Whitmer will continue to work to get the job done so we can protect our lakes and power economic growth for generations to come," LaRouche added.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c50f65/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2693x1511+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F06%2Fa970c6494106bf21aeadd823b70c%2Fasiancarp-08xx25-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Carp jump from the water of a river" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c50f65/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2693x1511+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F06%2Fa970c6494106bf21aeadd823b70c%2Fasiancarp-08xx25-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15943cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2693x1511+0+142/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F06%2Fa970c6494106bf21aeadd823b70c%2Fasiancarp-08xx25-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c50f65/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2693x1511+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F06%2Fa970c6494106bf21aeadd823b70c%2Fasiancarp-08xx25-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Silver carp jump out of the waters of the Illinois River.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The long-delayed barricade project would lodge a series of deterrent technologies — including an electric barrier, acoustic blasts, a bubble curtain and a special lock capable of flushing out the carp — into a crucial choke point in the Des Plaines River.</p><p>The invasive carp, particularly the silver carp and bighead carp, can out-eat and out-compete native fish and have already transformed the Illinois River and parts of the Mississippi River. Experts, industry leaders and local officials worry that if carp make it past Chicago’s shipping channel, the fish could massively disrupt the region’s multibillion-dollar fishing and recreational industries.</p><p>Illinois, Michigan and the Army Corps signed a deal to execute the project on July 1, 2024. As part of the agreement, Illinois committed approximately $50 million which has been used for design and early construction work. The state also acquired land for the project at the request of the Army Corps. Some of that real estate may be impacted by legacy coal ash pollution, which the state will also have to pay to remediate, according to the deal.</p><p>The big ticket project has had a bumpy road. Last February, Pritzker put the project on hold citing concerns the Trump administration could not deliver on its financial commitments after it withheld funds promised to Illinois under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.</p><p>Later that spring, President Trump signed a memo announcing his support to keep the carp out of Lake Michigan. Shortly afterward, the Army Corps confirmed that it had secured approximately $100 million for the first phase of construction which wrapped up last July.</p><p>Last December, the Trump administration issued a review of the project, effectively putting it on pause. Then last month, Trump posted on social media that he was working with Whitmer to “save the Great Lakes,” concluding that “only Trump can do it.”</p><p>WBEZ reached out to the Army Corps of Engineers, asking if the move from Illinois to Michigan means that funds for the project have been unfrozen and if construction is scheduled to begin again. They did not respond before publication.</p>
entries.author: Juanpablo Ramirez-Franco | WBEZ

entries.title: Illinois regulators have little power to stop hospital shutdowns, lawmakers are stepping in to change that
entries.summary: <p>When West Suburban Medical Center abruptly closed its doors temporarily last month, saying it had run out of cash to pay staff, there was little warning for thousands of patients and hundreds of employees.</p><p>Illinois regulators tasked with overseeing hospitals had little power to stop it. They also might not be able to keep open some of the dozens of other safety net hospitals around the state that mostly treat low-income and immigrant communities amid <a class="Link" href="https://www.kff.org/medicaid/medicaid-what-to-watch-in-2026/" target="_blank" ><u>federal changes next year</u></a> that could drastically reduce how much money medical centers get paid.</p><p>But two Democratic state lawmakers are proposing <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus/FullText?LegDocId=209492&DocName=10400SB3916sam001&DocNum=3916&DocTypeID=SB&LegID=167375&GAID=18&SessionID=114&SpecSess=&Session=" target="_blank" ><u>legislation</u></a> to help the state better prepare. Bills from state Sen. Laura Fine and state Rep. Robyn Gabel would require all hospitals to file plans with the state in case they close or scale back services to make sure patients don’t fall through the cracks. For-profit hospitals owned by investors, like West Suburban, also would have to give the state a deeper look into their finances. Now, that’s largely shielded from the public.</p><p>“The state, with legislation like this, is really stepping up to make sure that our hospital system is strong and that people are able to get the care that they need … so we are really ready for whatever happens in the future,” said Fine, who represents the northern suburbs and is sponsoring the Senate bill.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-df0000" name="image-df0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6a0dbc3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a185255/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A portrait of State Sen. Laura Fine" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a24375c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Sen. Laura Fine is proposing legislation that she hopes will better protect patients if there is a closure and requires hospitals to plan ahead.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Rich Hein/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The proposed legislation was filed in February. Fine said she doesn’t know if it would have prevented West Suburban’s closure, “but it would have prepared us for it, and a plan would have been in place once that process started.”</p><p>When the Oak Park hospital suddenly closed, several lawmakers called for better state oversight, especially considering the millions of taxpayer dollars hospitals receive to treat patients and pay for operations.</p><p>“We do need to have a warning system,” said Democratic state Rep. Norma Hernandez, whose district includes West Suburban and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2022/3/10/22970989/westlake-hospital-owner-sell-chicago-area-hospitals-pipeline-health" target="_blank" ><u>Westlake Hospital</u></a>, which closed in 2019. “What they’re doing now, it should be illegal because it’s so harmful.”</p><p>She recommends tracking public dollars hospitals receive to understand how they’re spent, such as if they’re paying their bills on time.</p><p>Dr. Manoj Prasad, West Suburban’s owner and CEO of Resilience Healthcare, said he told state officials for months that he needed support and more money. But he owes <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2026/04/01/west-suburban-medical-center-struggled-treat-patients-closed-vows-to-reopen-weiss-memorial-inspection-reports-oak-park" target="_blank" ><u>more than $100 million</u></a> to the state and vendors. A financial consultant reviewed the hospital’s finances, but it’s not complete, a state spokeswoman said, alleging the hospital hadn’t provided full transparency into its finances and operations.</p><p>Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/ftp/JCAR/AdminCode/077/077011300B02400R.html" target="_blank" ><u>followed the law</u></a> when he suspended services at West Suburban. He said it’s temporary. He had 30 days to notify the state and local lawmakers after suspending services and explain why. Now he must provide updates every 30 days.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bc0000" name="image-bc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/200e752/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27cc906/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Dr. Manoj Prasad, who owns West Suburban Medical Center, speaks before a bank of microphones at the hospital in April." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/63e8885/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dr. Manoj Prasad, who owns West Suburban Medical Center, blames a new electronic medical record system for the hospital’s financial woes. Prasad said he had to suspend services because the system failed to bill for much of the hospital’s work for the last year. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Dr. Linda Rae Murray, a retired local physician, understands how little power the state has to regulate hospitals. She was on the Illinois Health Facilities and Services Review Board, which regulates the health care industry, when Mercy Hospital on the Near South Side <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2021/10/25/inside-the-fight-to-save-chicagos-historic-mercy-hospital" target="_blank" ><u>planned to shutter</u></a> during the pandemic, before getting bought for $1. It’s now called Insight Hospital and Medical Center.</p><p>The board <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2020/12/15/state-regulators-say-mercy-hospital-must-stay-open-for-now" target="_blank" ><u>voted against Mercy’s closure</u></a>, but <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2021/03/23/historic-mercy-hospital-can-be-sold-the-next-hurdle-is-reviving-it" target="_blank" ><u>had to approve</u></a> the new owner. All it required was the new owner filling out paperwork correctly. Murray said she was so frustrated by the board’s broader policies and procedures that she left when her term was up.</p><p>She understands the frustration over West Suburban’s sudden closure. People have deep ties to their community hospital, and it’s often the biggest employer in the area.</p><p>But, Murray said, “I don’t understand how anyone can expect any state facility board or any other function of state government to force a hospital to stay open if, in fact, the hospital is losing money.”</p><p>She has an idea for how the state could toughen up: require hospitals to keep reserves for emergencies, like making payroll, and keep a closer eye on the quality of care provided.</p><p>Inspectors visited West Suburban <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2026/04/01/west-suburban-medical-center-struggled-treat-patients-closed-vows-to-reopen-weiss-memorial-inspection-reports-oak-park" target="_blank" ><u>several times</u></a> over patient care issues before the hospital closed. Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" target="_blank" ><u>closed his other hospital</u></a>, Weiss Memorial in Uptown on Chicago’s North Side, last August.</p><h3><b>Bill would make all hospitals develop a ‘financial resolution plan’ </b></h3><p>Fine’s proposed legislation, which Gabel mirrors in the state House, would require all hospitals licensed by the state to submit a “financial resolution plan” in the event of “material financial distress.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-110000" name="image-110000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/35e0ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f97c14d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A sign at West Suburban Medical Center on April 1 alerting patients of its closure." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d313012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A sign at West Suburban Medical Center on April 1 alerting patients of its closure.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The plans would include everything from who owns the hospital, to how to safely transfer patients to other hospitals if their owners temporarily suspend services or close. Hospitals would need to identify potential gaps in services they would create, and what would happen to their employees and medical trainees.</p><p>Hospitals that don’t comply would be fined no more than $500 a week, or just over $25,000 a year.</p><p>For for-profit hospitals, there would be even more requirements: audited financial statements and monthly balance sheets, among other documents, filed with the state every quarter that could provide clues into whether those medical centers are struggling. If they don’t comply, the Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services, which oversees Medicaid public health insurance for people who are low-income or have a disability, could cut off funding.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.kff.org/state-health-policy-data/state-indicator/hospitals-by-ownership/?currentTimeframe=0&sortModel=%7B%22colId%22:%22Location%22,%22sort%22:%22asc%22%7D" target="_blank" ><u>majority of hospitals </u></a>in Illinois are nonprofits, according to KFF, a health policy and research organization. Their finances are easier to find than a for-profit, from public tax returns filed with the government, to hospitals posting audited financial statements online.</p><p>Prasad said it would be too expensive to file audits each quarter. But he said it’s fair for the state to want to look deeper.</p><p>Fine hopes her legislation doesn’t get watered down during negotiations with the Illinois Health and Hospital Association, a powerful lobbyist for almost every hospital in the state and others.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1bf606c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/17ba6d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Representative Robyn Gabel speaks at a podium on July 29, 2024. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/17f1f74/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Rep. Robyn Gabel is sponsoring the legislation in the state House to try to hold hospitals more accountable.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A.J. Wilhelmi, president and CEO of the IHA, said some of the information that would be required is already reported to the state, such as how many licensed beds hospitals have.</p><p>“If the goal here is to have a better understanding of hospitals that are struggling so that there can be continuity of care in the event that service lines or hospitals close, let’s have that conversation,” Wilhelmi said. “But I think broadening that scope to the entire hospital community is challenging.”</p><p>Spokespeople for HFS and the Illinois public health department said the two agencies support the proposed legislation.</p><p><i>Contributing: Kaitlin Washburn</i></p>
